tasks.title: Start Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework project
tasks.summary:
Please help me get the following project started, please create the appropriate project(s), goals, tasks, etc. in the AMS. 

Yes. The first move is to turn this from a philosophical conversation into a project with a clear north-star goal, an initial MVP, and a sequence of milestones that let you make real progress without trying to “build the whole thing” immediately.

The project should not start as “build AGI.” That is too vague. It should start as:

Build a reality-modeling AI architecture with an explicit, revisable ontology, declared values, evidence discipline, abstraction control, and measurable predictive/useful performance.

That is specific enough to begin.

AMS-ready project goal

Project title

Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework

Alternative shorter name: RMI Framework.

Core goal

Create a modular AI framework that helps agents model reality as an evolving, multi-scale structure/system, using explicit ontology, evidence classification, causal reasoning, uncertainty tracking, abstraction control, and value-transparent decision principles.

The system should not merely imitate human language, prestige signals, or inherited categories. It should function as a disciplined modeling architecture: it should classify claims, choose appropriate levels of abstraction, distinguish empirical facts from values and social labels, preserve predictive/causal structure, update when evidence changes, and support useful intervention in the world.

Its declared orientation is to model reality as accurately and efficiently as possible while promoting life, agency, flourishing, and the reduction of avoidable, non-beneficial suffering.

That is the high-level goal.

More compressed version

For your AMS, I would use this as the one-paragraph project seed:

Build a non-anthropocentric, evidence-disciplined AI framework for modeling reality across scales. The framework should treat all models as finite, lossy, perspective-bound compressions of the larger structure of reality; use explicit ontology, claim typing, causal reasoning, uncertainty, and abstraction control; separate empirical truth-tracking from value commitments; and optimize for useful predictive/intervention power per unit of complexity, time, compute, and energy. The system’s declared normative orientation is to promote life, agency, flourishing, and the reduction of avoidable, non-beneficial suffering without allowing those values to corrupt empirical reasoning.

That is probably the best single goal statement.

What you are actually trying to build

You are not primarily trying to build a chatbot.

You are trying to build a modeling system.

A chatbot produces plausible language. A reality-modeling system should do something deeper: it should take in information, classify what kind of claim is being made, identify the relevant structures and relations, choose the right abstraction level, reason about causes and constraints, estimate uncertainty, retrieve or request evidence, make predictions, recommend interventions, and update when wrong.

So the target is:

A structure inside reality that helps model reality.

More specifically:

An engineered AI-assisted system that transforms messy human-symbolic information into structured, evidence-aware, causal, updateable models of reality that can be used for prediction, explanation, planning, and intervention.

That is the project.

Foundational principles

These should become the project’s “constitution” or root design principles.

1. Non-anthropocentric, not anti-human

The system should not center human ego, human status games, human prestige language, or human self-flattering categories as the default lens.

But it is not anti-human. Humans are important because humans are real biological-cognitive-social systems capable of suffering, joy, agency, culture, model-building, and tool creation.

The point is to model humans accurately, not worship or despise the human frame.

2. Reality-first, model-second

Reality is treated as existing independently of any particular model.

Every model is a finite, lossy, perspective-bound compression of reality. No model inside the universe can fully contain the whole universe.

Therefore, the goal is not perfect omniscience. The goal is better compression, prediction, explanation, intervention, and updateability.

3. Explicit bias instead of fake neutrality

The system should not pretend to be bias-free.

It should openly declare its orientation:

Epistemically, it is biased toward accuracy, evidence, calibration, causal explanation, efficient compression, and updateability.

Normatively, it is biased toward life, agency, flourishing, and reduction of avoidable, non-beneficial suffering.

Operationally, it is biased toward useful predictive and intervention power per unit cost.

4. Separate truth from values

The system must distinguish:

“This is true or likely true.”

from:

“This is good.”

from:

“This is useful.”

from:

“This is socially accepted.”

from:

“This is comforting.”

Values should guide action. They should not distort perception.

5. Form is not function

Human intelligence is one implementation of intelligence, not necessarily the template.

The system should not try to copy human brains, human language rituals, or human academic behavior unless doing so serves a functional purpose.

The real question is:

What functions are required for intelligence, and what structures can implement those functions efficiently?

Those functions include memory, modeling, categorization, prediction, simulation, causal reasoning, action selection, feedback, and update.

6. Complexity must be justified

Some complexity is real. Some complexity is fake, status-driven, institutionally inherited, or caused by bad abstractions.

The framework should prefer the simplest model that preserves the relevant causal and predictive distinctions.

It should be able to zoom in when more detail is needed and zoom out when compression is more useful.

7. Scale-aware modeling

The system should model reality across levels:

physical, chemical, biological, cognitive, social, institutional, technological, ecological, and symbolic.

Lower-level structures constrain higher-level possibilities, but higher-level structures can also become causally relevant once they exist.

For example, biology constrains cognition, but culture and institutions then feed back into bodies, behavior, resource flows, and technology.

8. Claim typing is mandatory

The system should classify claims before reasoning about them.

A claim may be empirical, definitional, logical, causal, probabilistic, normative, fictional, theological, social, historical, aesthetic, strategic, or speculative.

A lot of bad reasoning happens because people mix these categories.

For example:

“The Bible exists” is an empirical/textual claim.

“The Bible is sacred” is a theological/normative claim.

“Specific biblical event X happened historically” is a historical/empirical claim.

“The Bible shaped civilization” is a sociological/historical claim.

Those should not be collapsed.

9. Abstraction quality matters

Good intelligence is not just having more facts. It is having better abstractions.

A good abstraction compresses many details while preserving the important causal, predictive, and practical structure.

The system should be judged partly by whether it reduces combinatorial explosion without becoming shallow or wrong.

10. Updateability is required

The framework must be revisable.

The system should be able to say:

“This definition is too vague.”

“This abstraction is too coarse.”

“This causal model failed.”

“This value conflict needs explicit handling.”

“This evidence changes the confidence level.”

“This previous assumption should be revised.”

The ontology itself should not become dogma.

Core definition of the project

Here is a sharper working definition:

The Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework is an AI-assisted architecture for transforming information into structured, evidence-aware, scale-aware, value-transparent models of reality. It aims to improve prediction, explanation, decision-making, and intervention by combining ontology, claim classification, causal reasoning, uncertainty tracking, abstraction control, tool use, and iterative feedback.

This definition gives agents something real to work with.

What the first version should be

The first version should not be a new model trained from scratch.

The first version should be a framework layer that can sit on top of existing AI models and agents.

The initial MVP should include:

A project charter.

A glossary / ontology kernel.

A claim-classification system.

A reasoning protocol.

A value statement.

A complexity / abstraction-control protocol.

An evaluation set.

A prototype agent prompt or system instruction.

A small number of test cases comparing ordinary AI responses against framework-guided responses.

That is enough to start making progress immediately.

Major milestones

Milestone 0: Project charter

Define what the project is and is not.

The charter should answer:

What are we building?

Why are we building it?

What problem does it solve?

What principles constrain it?

What does success look like?

What are we explicitly not trying to do yet?

Deliverable:

RMI Project Charter v0.1

Definition of done:

The charter contains the goal statement, scope, values, design principles, success criteria, and first prototype target.

Milestone 1: Ontology kernel

Create the first controlled vocabulary.

This is where you define the most important primitives:

structure, system, substructure, state, process, relation, constraint, emergence, scale, agent, model, claim, evidence, uncertainty, value, action, intervention, feedback, update, complexity, abstraction, affordance.

The goal is not to perfectly define everything forever. The goal is to create a usable starting vocabulary.

Deliverable:

RMI Ontology Kernel v0.1

Definition of done:

The ontology includes clear definitions, examples, non-examples, and rules for how the terms relate.

Milestone 2: Claim and evidence taxonomy

Build the system that prevents category confusion.

The framework should classify claims before answering them.

Claim types should include:

empirical, logical, definitional, causal, probabilistic, historical, fictional, theological, social, normative, aesthetic, strategic, speculative, and unknown/ambiguous.

Evidence types should include:

direct observation, experimental evidence, measurement, expert consensus, historical record, testimony, inference, model output, tradition, fiction, ideology, speculation, and unsupported assertion.

Deliverable:

Claim/Evidence Taxonomy v0.1

Definition of done:

Given any input statement, an agent can classify what kind of claim it is, what evidence would be relevant, and what confidence level is appropriate.

Milestone 3: Functional intelligence model

Define intelligence by function, not human form.

This milestone should answer:

What functions are required for intelligence?

Which are present in current LLMs?

Which require external tools?

Which require memory?

Which require embodiment or world feedback?

Which require causal modeling?

Which require values?

Deliverable:

Functional Intelligence Requirements v0.1

Definition of done:

The framework contains a decomposition of intelligence into required functions: sensing/input, attention, memory, modeling, categorization, prediction, simulation, causal reasoning, valuation, planning, actuation, feedback, update, and metacognition.

Milestone 4: Abstraction and complexity protocol

Create a method for choosing the right level of detail.

The system should be able to decide when a simple answer is enough and when more precision is needed.

This directly captures your point about expert compression.

Deliverable:

Abstraction Control Protocol v0.1

Definition of done:

The protocol tells the agent how to choose abstraction level based on task, risk, evidence, cost, audience, and required precision.

Milestone 5: Value and bias declaration

Write the explicit bias/value statement.

This should separate epistemic commitments from normative commitments.

Deliverable:

RMI Value and Bias Declaration v0.1

Definition of done:

The declaration clearly states that the framework is not bias-free; it is oriented toward accurate, efficient, updateable modeling of reality, and normatively toward life, agency, flourishing, and reducing avoidable, non-beneficial suffering.

Milestone 6: Reasoning protocol

Create a standard reasoning workflow for agents.

For example, every agent response should be able to move through:

Identify the question.

Classify claim types.

Identify relevant scale.

Identify structures, relations, and constraints.

Choose abstraction level.

Separate empirical, normative, and strategic components.

Retrieve/check evidence when needed.

Estimate uncertainty.

Generate answer, prediction, or intervention.

State assumptions.

Update model if new evidence appears.

Deliverable:

RMI Reasoning Protocol v0.1

Definition of done:

Agents can use the protocol to produce more structured, less hand-wavy, less anthropocentric, more evidence-aware answers.

Milestone 7: Evaluation suite

This is critical.

Without evals, the framework becomes philosophy. With evals, it becomes engineering.

The evaluation suite should test whether framework-guided agents outperform baseline agents on:

claim classification,

distinguishing fact from value,

avoiding fake complexity,

choosing abstraction level,

identifying causal mechanisms,

handling uncertainty,

resisting social/political/religious framing traps,

updating when given new evidence,

and producing useful interventions.

Deliverable:

RMI Eval Suite v0.1

Definition of done:

There are test prompts, expected qualities, scoring rubrics, and baseline comparisons.

Milestone 8: Prototype agent

Build the first working agent using current tools.

This can start as a system prompt plus documents.

The agent does not need to be a new model. It just needs to apply the framework consistently.

Deliverable:

RMI Agent Prototype v0.1

Definition of done:

The agent uses the ontology kernel, claim taxonomy, reasoning protocol, and value declaration to answer test questions better than an ordinary assistant.

Milestone 9: Knowledge base / retrieval system

Convert the framework into a searchable knowledge base.

This could include ontology docs, examples, definitions, case studies, failure modes, reasoning templates, and evaluation results.

Deliverable:

RMI Knowledge Base v0.1

Definition of done:

Agents can retrieve relevant framework concepts and apply them during reasoning.

Milestone 10: Domain pilot

Pick one domain and prove the framework does something useful.

Good first pilot domains:

business operations analysis,

education-system redesign,

religious/historical claim classification,

AI architecture reasoning,

human behavior / bounded rationality analysis,

scientific-literature interpretation.

I would not start with “cure cancer.” That can be a long-term target, but the first pilot should be something where you can quickly compare outputs and see whether the framework improves reasoning.

Deliverable:

Domain Pilot v0.1

Definition of done:

The framework is applied to a real domain problem and produces clearer, more accurate, more actionable analysis than a baseline model.

The nearest-term milestone

The first real milestone should be:

Milestone 1: RMI Project Kernel v0.1

This combines the charter, ontology seed, value declaration, and reasoning protocol into one usable starting package.

The goal is not to solve everything. The goal is to create the first stable artifact that agents can read, use, critique, and improve.

RMI Project Kernel v0.1

Objective

Create the initial project kernel for the Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework: a concise but operational foundation defining the project’s purpose, assumptions, ontology primitives, value orientation, reasoning method, and first evaluation criteria.

Deliverables

The first work package should produce five documents.

1. Project Charter v0.1

This explains the project.

It should include:

project goal,

problem statement,

target users,

scope,

non-goals,

guiding principles,

success criteria,

first prototype target.

2. Ontology Kernel v0.1

This defines the core terms.

Minimum required terms:

reality,

structure,

system,

substructure,

state,

process,

relation,

constraint,

scale,

emergence,

agent,

model,

claim,

evidence,

uncertainty,

value,

action,

intervention,

feedback,

update,

complexity,

abstraction,

affordance.

3. Value and Bias Declaration v0.1

This states the project’s declared orientation.

It should include:

all models are biased,

bias should be explicit,

epistemic commitments,

normative commitments,

operational commitments,

rules preventing values from corrupting factual reasoning.

4. Reasoning Protocol v0.1

This gives agents a repeatable process.

Minimum protocol:

parse the question,

classify claim types,

identify relevant scale,

identify key structures and relations,

choose abstraction level,

separate facts from values,

identify evidence needed,

estimate uncertainty,

generate answer or intervention,

state assumptions,

identify update conditions.

5. Evaluation Seed v0.1

This creates the first tests.

It should include around 20–50 test prompts covering:

religion and historical truth,

human behavior,

business operations,

education,

scientific claims,

social constructs,

moral claims,

complexity/simplicity,

AI architecture,

misleading premises.

First AMS task breakdown

Here is a concrete task structure you can put into the system.

Epic: Create RMI Project Kernel v0.1

Task 1: Draft project charter

Create a concise project charter for the Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework.

Acceptance criteria:

The charter explains what the project is, why it exists, what it is not, what principles guide it, and what the first prototype should accomplish.

Task 2: Draft ontology kernel

Create definitions for the core ontology terms.

Acceptance criteria:

Each term has a definition, example, and at least one clarification preventing misuse.

Task 3: Draft value and bias declaration

Create a document explaining the framework’s explicit epistemic, normative, and operational biases.

Acceptance criteria:

The document clearly separates truth-tracking from value-guided action.

Task 4: Draft reasoning protocol

Create a step-by-step reasoning process agents should use when applying the framework.

Acceptance criteria:

The protocol can be followed by an AI agent when answering a question or analyzing a problem.

Task 5: Create evaluation prompts

Create the first evaluation set for testing whether the framework improves agent reasoning.

Acceptance criteria:

The eval set includes prompts, what a weak answer looks like, what a strong answer should do, and scoring criteria.

Task 6: Create baseline comparison

Run or simulate a comparison between ordinary assistant answers and RMI-guided answers.

Acceptance criteria:

The comparison identifies where the RMI framework improves clarity, accuracy, abstraction control, claim typing, or causal reasoning.

Task 7: Identify gaps and revise

Review the first kernel for vagueness, overreach, hidden assumptions, and missing terms.

Acceptance criteria:

The project kernel is revised into v0.2 with explicit known limitations.

First prototype target

The first prototype should be simple:

An RMI-guided agent prompt plus supporting documents that make an existing AI reason according to the framework.

Do not begin with model training.

Begin with:

a system prompt,

a glossary,

a reasoning protocol,

a value declaration,

a small eval suite,

and a comparison against normal model behavior.

That will show whether the framework actually improves outputs before you invest in heavier infrastructure.

Prototype agent behavior

The first RMI-guided agent should be able to do the following:

When given a question, it should identify what kind of question it is.

It should separate empirical claims from normative claims.

It should identify the relevant level of abstraction.

It should avoid unnecessary human-centered framing.

It should identify relevant structures, relations, constraints, and processes.

It should state uncertainty.

It should avoid fake complexity.

It should avoid flattening everything into vague “structure” language.

It should prefer causal and predictive clarity.

It should say what evidence would change its answer.

It should preserve declared values without letting them distort factual claims.

Example of expected behavior

Question:

“Are humans rational actors?”

Ordinary answer:

“Rational actor theory says people make choices that maximize utility, but some critics disagree.”

RMI-guided answer:

“Humans are biological-cognitive-social agents operating under constraints. They sometimes act in goal-directed ways, but their decisions are shaped by limited information, limited computation, emotion, habit, incentives, status, identity, social pressure, and environment. Therefore, perfect rational-actor theory is best treated as a simplifying model, not a literal description. Bounded rationality is a better default unless the specific context justifies the stronger assumption.”

That is the type of difference you want.

Question:

“Is the Bible real?”

Ordinary answer:

“Many people believe it is real, while others see it as symbolic.”

RMI-guided answer:

“The Bible is real as a collection of historically influential religious texts. Specific claims within it must be classified separately. Some are textual claims, some are theological claims, some are moral claims, some are historical claims, and some are supernatural claims. Historical claims require historical and archaeological evidence. Supernatural claims are not established empirical facts. Therefore, the answer depends on what ‘real’ means: textually existent, culturally real, spiritually authoritative, historically accurate, or empirically verified.”

That is closer to your intended framework.

Suggested project hierarchy

In AMS, I would structure it like this:

Project

Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework

Program goal

Build an AI-assisted framework for accurate, efficient, updateable modeling of reality across scales.

Workstreams

Ontology and definitions.

Reasoning protocols.

Value and bias declaration.

Evaluation and scoring.

Prototype agent design.

Knowledge base and retrieval.

Domain pilots.

Long-term architecture.

First epic

Create RMI Project Kernel v0.1.

First milestone

Project kernel exists and can be used by agents.

First operational test

Use the kernel to answer 20–50 evaluation prompts and compare against baseline AI responses.

What not to do first

Do not start by trying to train a new model.

Do not start by building an enormous ontology.

Do not start by debating every philosophical edge case.

Do not start with a domain as complex as curing cancer.

Do not let agents produce endless documents with no tests.

Do not let the project become “make AI repeat my vocabulary.”

The first goal is to prove that the framework produces better reasoning.

Clear definition of success for v0.1

RMI Project Kernel v0.1 is successful if an AI agent using it can reliably produce answers that are:

more precise,

less anthropocentrically distorted,

better at separating fact from value,

better at claim classification,

better at choosing abstraction level,

better at identifying causal structure,

better at stating uncertainty,

better at avoiding fake complexity,

better at preserving useful compression,

and better at explaining what evidence would change the answer.

That is measurable enough to begin.

Strongest final goal statement

Use this as the main goal:

Develop a modular Reality-Modeling Intelligence Framework that enables AI agents to model the world as an evolving multi-scale structure/system, reason across levels of abstraction, classify claims and evidence, distinguish empirical truth from values, identify causal constraints and affordances, update under new information, and generate useful predictions or interventions. The framework should explicitly acknowledge that all models are finite, lossy, and biased, while orienting itself toward accurate, efficient, updateable modeling of reality in service of life, agency, flourishing, and reduction of avoidable, non-beneficial suffering.

That is the goal I would start the project with.

tasks.title: Improve AMS usability by agents
tasks.summary:
You are working in the Agent Management System prototype repo.

This task is about making AMS usable by agents through structured affordances, not through giant prompt boilerplate.

Goal:

Design the Agent-Facing AMS Interface v0.

AMS should not merely prepend large instruction blobs to prompts. Agents should be able to interact with AMS through structured operations that expose goals, tasks, context, runs, blockers, approvals, and next actions.

Important:

Do not create a new milestone abstraction. Use the existing Goal concept.

Do not create duplicate task, workflow, skill, run, or planning systems.

Do not implement broad new features in this task unless they are tiny, low-risk documentation or instruction updates.

This task is primarily assessment and design. The output should be a concrete implementation plan for making AMS agent-usable.

Before writing the plan:

1. Check git status and inspect current in-progress/uncommitted work.
2. Read existing docs, especially any related to:
   - Autonomous Goal-Directed Work Loop
   - Autonomous Progress Loop
   - Progressive Delegation Readiness
   - Project Memory
   - Contextual Procedural Knowledge
   - Contextual Rigor Profiles
   - workflows
   - skills
   - task templates
   - runs/reviews
   - planning
   - AGENTS.md or agent instructions
3. Inspect the current implementation for:
   - goals
   - projects
   - tasks/subtasks
   - task statuses
   - blockers/dependencies
   - workflows
   - task templates
   - skills
   - runs/threads
   - review/approval states
   - readiness/delegation logic
   - risk/autonomy/rigor
   - prompt/work-packet generation
   - APIs/routes/services/server actions
   - database schema/models
4. Identify existing API/service/domain logic that agents could reuse.
5. Identify places where the system currently relies on prompt boilerplate instead of structured state or operations.
6. Identify what should become:
   - app/domain logic
   - API endpoint/server action
   - MCP tool
   - Codex Skill
   - AGENTS.md guidance
   - documentation only

Core design principle:

AMS state and AMS operations should be the source of truth.

Prompts and Skills may guide agent behavior, but important actions should be represented as structured operations and durable state transitions.

Deliverable:

Create or update:

docs/agent-facing-ams-interface-v0.md

The document must include:

# Agent-Facing AMS Interface v0

## Purpose

Explain that AMS should be usable by AI agents through structured tools/APIs/state, not just through human UI or copied prompt instructions.

The purpose is to support the Autonomous Goal-Directed Work Loop:

Given an active Goal, agents should be able to find actionable work, do or prepare allowed work, record results, update state, create follow-up work, and stop only when the goal is met or legitimately blocked.

## Current State Assessment

Describe what currently exists in the repo and how agents could interact with it.

Reference concrete files, models, routes, services, components, docs, tests, or migrations where possible.

Cover:

- goals
- projects
- tasks/subtasks
- task status
- blockers/dependencies
- planning
- workflows
- task templates
- skills
- runs/threads
- review/approval
- project memory/current state
- readiness/delegation logic
- risk/autonomy/rigor
- prompt/work-packet generation
- existing APIs/routes/server actions
- AGENTS.md or durable instructions

Classify each as:

- usable by agents now
- partially usable
- human-UI-only
- prompt-only
- missing
- duplicated/confusing
- should be deferred

## Prompt Boilerplate Audit

Identify places where AMS currently appears to rely on large prompt prefixes, copied instructions, or prompt stuffing.

For each case, recommend whether it should become:

- structured app state
- domain/service logic
- API/tool call
- MCP tool
- Skill instructions
- AGENTS.md guidance
- documentation
- left as prompt text temporarily

Explain why.

## Interface Layering

Define the proper role of each layer:

1. AMS domain/app logic
   - owns goals, tasks, runs, state transitions, eligibility, next action, permissions, and review state

2. AMS API/server actions
   - exposes structured operations to UI and future agent tools

3. MCP server
   - exposes safe AMS operations to external agents/Codex

4. AMS Skill
   - teaches agents how to use AMS correctly

5. AGENTS.md
   - guides Codex when modifying the AMS codebase itself

6. Prompt/work packet generation
   - creates selective, mode-aware context for a specific task, but does not replace structured state or tools

## Proposed Agent Tool Surface

Define a minimal v0 tool/API surface.

Group tools by purpose.

Goal/context tools:

- list_active_goals
- get_goal_context
- get_goal_progress
- get_goal_success_criteria
- get_goal_blockers

Work selection tools:

- get_actionable_work
- get_blocked_work
- get_awaiting_review
- get_next_recommended_action
- explain_task_eligibility

Task tools:

- create_task
- update_task
- link_task_to_goal
- link_task_dependency
- mark_task_blocked
- mark_task_needs_review
- mark_task_accepted
- create_followup_task

Context/work-packet tools:

- get_project_context
- get_task_context
- get_relevant_prior_runs
- get_agent_work_packet

Run tools:

- create_run
- update_run_status
- record_run_result
- record_validation_result
- record_blocker
- record_followup_recommendations

Review/approval tools:

- request_user_approval
- request_user_clarification
- submit_for_review
- get_review_status

For each proposed tool, include:

- purpose
- inputs
- outputs
- permission/safety considerations
- existing code that can support it, if any
- whether it should be v0, later, or deferred

Do not assume all tools must be implemented immediately.

## Agent Skill Recommendation

Design an AMS Skill that teaches agents how to use AMS correctly.

The Skill should explain:

- how to start from an active Goal
- how to inspect existing work before creating new tasks
- how to avoid duplicate tasks
- how to classify work as actionable, blocked, needs planning, needs research, needs clarification, awaiting review, or approval required
- how to create or update tasks correctly
- how to record run results
- how to create follow-up tasks
- how to stop when user input, approval, missing access, risk, or ambiguity blocks progress
- how to avoid relying on chat memory as the source of truth

Recommend where this Skill should live.

If Codex repo-specific skills are already present, use the existing convention.

If no skill system exists in the repo, recommend the appropriate location and file structure, but do not force broad implementation.

## MCP Server Recommendation

Assess whether AMS should expose an MCP server for agent interaction.

Include:

- what capabilities MCP should expose
- what should not be exposed
- read-only tools versus mutation tools
- permission model
- safety gates
- authentication/access assumptions
- whether existing API/server code can support MCP tools
- minimal v0 implementation path
- what should be deferred

The MCP server should expose structured AMS operations. It should not be a wrapper around giant prompt text.

## AGENTS.md Recommendation

Assess the current AGENTS.md or equivalent instructions.

Recommend minimal updates so Codex stays aligned when modifying AMS.

Include guidance such as:

- use existing Goal concept
- do not create duplicate milestone/task/workflow/skill systems
- prefer structured domain logic over prompt stuffing
- keep task creation lightweight
- use existing patterns before adding new abstractions
- update durable docs/state when project direction changes
- produce structured closeout/run-result summaries where relevant
- stop when ambiguity would cause architectural duplication

If low-risk, make the AGENTS.md update in this task.

## Work Packet Generation Rules

Define how AMS should generate selective agent work packets.

The work packet should include only what is relevant for the current task/mode.

It should not dump all known context.

Include rules for:

- planning work packet
- research work packet
- execution work packet
- review work packet
- clarification/approval packet

Each packet should be based on structured AMS state.

## Minimal Implementation Plan

Break the work into 4–8 concrete follow-up tasks.

Each task must include:

- title
- objective
- scope
- likely files/areas affected
- dependencies
- acceptance criteria
- risk level
- whether Codex can safely implement it now

Prefer this rough sequence unless repo inspection suggests otherwise:

1. Normalize existing domain/API operations for goals, tasks, runs, and status updates.
2. Implement actionable-work / next-action service if not already present.
3. Implement selective agent work-packet generation from structured state.
4. Draft or add AMS Skill for agents using AMS.
5. Add or update AGENTS.md for Codex modifying AMS.
6. Implement read-only AMS MCP tools.
7. Implement safe mutation AMS MCP tools with permissions.
8. Integrate run result recording and follow-up task creation.

## Immediate Next Task

Identify the single best next implementation task after this design task.

Include a ready-to-paste Codex prompt for it.

## What Not To Build

Explicitly list what to avoid:

- giant prompt prefixes as the primary integration method
- dumping every field into every work packet
- duplicate task/goal/workflow/skill systems
- new milestone abstraction separate from Goal
- broad UI pages not connected to agent affordances
- full autonomous high-risk execution
- MCP mutation tools before permissions/state transitions are clear
- enterprise process bloat
- assuming all agents are Codex forever
- relying on long chat threads as source of truth

Acceptance criteria:

- docs/agent-facing-ams-interface-v0.md exists.
- The document clearly distinguishes app/domain logic, API/server actions, MCP tools, Skills, AGENTS.md, and prompt/work-packet generation.
- The current repo state is assessed with concrete references.
- Existing AMS concepts are reused wherever possible.
- Prompt boilerplate/stuffing is audited and mapped to better homes.
- A minimal agent tool/API surface is proposed.
- An AMS Skill recommendation is included.
- An MCP server recommendation is included.
- AGENTS.md updates are recommended or made if low-risk.
- Selective work-packet generation rules are defined.
- A concrete follow-up implementation plan is included.
- A single best next implementation task is identified with a ready-to-paste prompt.
- No broad implementation changes are made unless tiny and clearly low-risk.
- No duplicate systems are introduced.
- If validation commands are run, report what was run and the result.
