tasks.title: Knowledge expansion across various domains and knowledge system enhancement
tasks.summary:
get started on what was discussed in the below chat:

Yes. The “affordance” piece is important enough that I would make it a first-class part of the system, not just a side note.

What you are describing is not only:

“What is this thing called?”

but also:

“What does this thing let me do, what is it good for, in what context, and why?”

That means your knowledge system should capture the relationship between:

Form → Material → Context → Task → Affordance → Tradeoff

For example:

Tool: Round-point shovel
Form: Curved blade with pointed tip
Material/structure: Stiff metal blade, long handle
Context: Soil, loose fill, planting, digging
Task: Penetrate, scoop, lift, move material
Affordance: Good for digging into soil and moving loose material
Tradeoff: Worse than a trenching shovel for narrow trenches; worse than a square shovel for scraping flat surfaces

That is different from a normal glossary entry. A glossary tells you what the tool is. An affordance-aware note tells you why that form exists.

For your Obsidian system, I would update the project from:

“Physical design vocabulary and glossary”

to something more like:

Physical World Concept System: Vocabulary, Forms, Affordances, Tools, Materials, and Cross-Domain Principles

That gives you room for terms like chamfer, object types like round-point shovel, operations like digging, and deeper concepts like penetration, leverage, shearing, scooping, flow control, edge geometry, and task fit.

Here is a stronger prompt you can give to Codex or another agent.

# Task: Build an Obsidian Knowledge System for Physical World Vocabulary, Affordances, and Cross-Domain Practical Concepts
I have an existing Obsidian vault with notes already in it. Your task is to inspect the existing vault, preserve what is already there, and create a contained, well-organized section for a new knowledge system focused on practical physical-world understanding.
This system should help me learn how to see, describe, design, use, repair, model, and reason about physical objects, spaces, materials, tools, and processes.
The system should not merely be a glossary. It should capture vocabulary, concept families, first principles, affordances, tool/task fit, material behavior, cross-domain patterns, and real-world observation cues.
## Core idea
For every term, object, tool, form, or process, capture not only:
- What is it called?
- What does it mean?
but also:
- What does it help me notice?
- What does it let me do?
- What is it good for?
- Why is it good for that?
- What is it bad at?
- What tradeoffs does its form or material create?
- What contexts make it useful or not useful?
- What deeper cross-domain principles does it illustrate?
The key model is:
Form → Material → Context → Task → Affordance → Tradeoff
Examples:
- A chamfer affords safer, less fragile, more visually controlled edges.
- A bullnose affords a rounded, comfortable, durable edge.
- A gambrel roof affords more usable attic/upper-floor volume while still shedding water.
- A round-point shovel affords digging and scooping in soil because its pointed curved blade penetrates and carries loose material.
- A square-point shovel affords scraping, edging, and moving loose material on flat surfaces.
- A chef’s knife affords rocking cuts, slicing, chopping, and controlled food preparation because of its blade geometry, length, handle placement, and edge profile.
- A French drain affords subsurface water redirection through slope, porous fill, and pipe/channel structure.
## Existing vault handling
Before creating new files:
1. Inspect the vault structure.
2. Identify existing folders, maps of content, tags, naming conventions, and note style.
3. Do not overwrite existing notes unless explicitly instructed.
4. If a note already exists for a term, either:
   - append a clearly marked section called `## Affordance and cross-domain analysis`, or
   - create a companion note and link both notes.
5. Prefer integrating with the vault’s existing conventions where obvious.
6. If the existing structure is unclear, create a contained folder so this work does not pollute the rest of the vault.
Create the new section under a folder such as:
`Knowledge/Physical World Concept System/`
or, if the vault already has a better location, use that and explain the choice.
## Desired folder structure
Create a contained structure similar to this:
```text
Physical World Concept System/
  00 MOCs/
    MOC - Physical World Concept System.md
    MOC - Physical Design Vocabulary.md
    MOC - Tools and Affordances.md
    MOC - Construction and Remodeling.md
    MOC - Landscaping and Exterior Spaces.md
    MOC - 3D Modeling and Object Design.md
    MOC - Cross-Domain Primitives.md
  10 Concept Families/
    Edge Treatments.md
    Surface Transitions.md
    Profiles and Cross-Sections.md
    Roof Forms.md
    Windows and Doors.md
    Trim and Millwork.md
    Drainage and Landscape Form.md
    Tools and Affordances.md
    Cutting and Separation.md
    Digging and Earthworking.md
    Joining and Fastening.md
    Layers and Assemblies.md
    Materials and Finishes.md
    Fit Tolerance Gaps and Reveals.md
    Wear Damage Failure and Repair.md
    3D Modeling Geometry.md
  20 Terms/
    Chamfer.md
    Bevel.md
    Bullnose.md
    Eased Edge.md
    Ogee.md
    Fillet.md
    Radius.md
    Gambrel Roof.md
    Swale.md
    French Drain.md
    Mullion.md
    Muntin.md
  30 Tools and Objects/
    Round-Point Shovel.md
    Square-Point Shovel.md
    Trenching Shovel.md
    Spade.md
    Chef Knife.md
    Utility Knife.md
    Handsaw.md
    Pry Bar.md
    Trowel.md
  40 Operations and Processes/
    Digging.md
    Scraping.md
    Cutting.md
    Slicing.md
    Chopping.md
    Prying.md
    Fastening.md
    Sealing.md
    Grading.md
    Drainage.md
    Blanching.md
    Reduction.md
  50 Cross-Domain Primitives/
    Modify Edge.md
    Remove Material.md
    Add Material.md
    Join Parts.md
    Separate Parts.md
    Penetrate Material.md
    Scoop and Carry.md
    Apply Leverage.md
    Control Flow.md
    Control Water.md
    Control Heat.md
    Concentrate.md
    Dilute.md
    Layer.md
    Seal.md
    Support Load.md
    Transfer Force.md
    Reduce Friction.md
    Increase Grip.md
    Protect Surface.md
    Finish Surface.md
  60 Observation Templates/
    Template - Term Note.md
    Template - Tool or Object Note.md
    Template - Operation Note.md
    Template - Affordance Analysis.md
    Template - Real World Observation.md

Adjust folder names if the vault already has a convention.

Knowledge architecture

Use the following note types:

1. MOC notes
    These are maps of content that orient the user.
2. Concept-family notes
    These explain a family of related terms, such as edge treatments, roof forms, cutting tools, or drainage systems.
3. Term notes
    These explain specific vocabulary terms such as chamfer, bullnose, gambrel roof, mullion, swale, or ogee.
4. Tool/object notes
    These explain specific tools or physical objects, with special attention to affordances.
5. Operation/process notes
    These explain actions such as cutting, digging, scraping, fastening, sealing, reducing, blanching, grading, or pruning.
6. Cross-domain primitive notes
    These explain reusable patterns that appear across many domains, such as cutting, joining, sealing, layering, concentrating, diluting, penetrating, scooping, supporting, shaping, smoothing, and controlling flow.
7. Observation notes
    These help me look at a room, building, yard, tool, object, or image and describe what I see using precise language.

Add affordances as a first-class concept

Create a central note:

Affordance.md

Explain affordance in practical terms:

An affordance is what an object, form, material, space, or tool makes possible, invites, enables, constrains, or makes easier for a user in a particular context.

The note should explain that affordances depend on:

* Shape
* Size
* Material
* Surface texture
* Weight
* Edge geometry
* Handle design
* Mechanical advantage
* User body mechanics
* Environmental context
* Task
* Skill level
* Safety constraints
* Maintenance requirements

Create links from Affordance.md to:

* Tools and Affordances.md
* Form.md
* Material.md
* Task Fit.md
* Tradeoff.md
* Constraint.md
* Ergonomics.md
* Failure Mode.md
* Cross-Domain Primitives.md

Required note template for term notes

Use this structure for individual vocabulary terms:

---
title:
aliases:
type: term
domains:
concept_families:
cross_domain_primitives:
related_terms:
status: seed
source_quality: needs review
---
# Term Name
## Short definition
Give a clear, plain-language definition.
## What it helps me notice
Explain what this term helps me see, identify, describe, or distinguish in the real world.
## First-principles explanation
Explain the underlying geometry, material behavior, construction logic, physical principle, design purpose, or process logic.
## Affordances
Explain what this form, feature, or concept enables.
Questions to answer:
- What does this make easier?
- What does this make safer?
- What does this make stronger, weaker, smoother, more durable, more visible, easier to manufacture, easier to maintain, or easier to use?
- What actions or outcomes does it invite?
## Tradeoffs and constraints
Explain what is gained and what is sacrificed.
## Common contexts
List where this appears in buildings, rooms, furniture, landscapes, tools, materials, 3D models, or other domains.
## Related terms and distinctions
Explain similar terms, contrasting terms, and commonly confused terms.
## Cross-domain connections
Explain where the same pattern appears in other domains.
## Visual examples to collect
Give 3–5 real-world examples I could photograph, sketch, model, or look up.
## Questions for further learning
Add questions that would deepen understanding.
## Sources
List sources used.
## Links
Add Obsidian links to related notes.

Required note template for tool/object notes

Use this structure for tools and physical objects:

---
title:
aliases:
type: tool_object
domains:
tool_families:
materials:
primary_operations:
affordances:
cross_domain_primitives:
related_terms:
status: seed
source_quality: needs review
---
# Tool or Object Name
## Short description
What is this tool/object?
## Primary tasks
What is it mainly used for?
## Affordance summary
In one paragraph, explain what this tool/object makes possible or easier.
## Form and geometry
Describe its shape, proportions, edge geometry, handle geometry, working surfaces, points, curves, angles, thicknesses, and other important form features.
## Materials and construction
Describe common materials and how those materials affect performance.
## Why it works
Explain the practical first principles behind the tool/object.
Consider:
- Force
- Leverage
- Pressure
- Edge geometry
- Friction
- Grip
- Mechanical advantage
- Weight
- Balance
- Stiffness
- Flexibility
- Durability
- Surface contact
- Penetration
- Scooping
- Cutting
- Scraping
- Prying
- Flow control
- Heat transfer
## Best contexts
Where does this tool/object perform well?
## Poor contexts
Where does this tool/object perform poorly?
## Tradeoffs
What does this design optimize for, and what does it sacrifice?
## Similar tools or objects
Compare with nearby alternatives.
For example:
- Round-point shovel vs square-point shovel vs trenching shovel vs spade
- Chef knife vs paring knife vs cleaver vs serrated knife
- Pry bar vs claw hammer vs wrecking bar
- Trowel vs putty knife vs float
- Handsaw vs backsaw vs pruning saw
## Failure modes and misuse
How does this tool fail, wear out, become unsafe, or get misused?
## Real-world recognition cues
How can I recognize this tool/object visually?
## Cross-domain connections
What deeper principles does this illustrate?
## Sources
List sources used.
## Links
Add Obsidian links to related notes.

Required note template for operation/process notes

Use this structure for actions, operations, and processes:

---
title:
aliases:
type: operation_process
domains:
process_families:
inputs:
outputs:
tools_used:
cross_domain_primitives:
related_terms:
status: seed
source_quality: needs review
---
# Operation or Process Name
## Short definition
What is this operation?
## Purpose
Why is it done?
## Inputs and outputs
What does the process start with, and what does it produce?
## Mechanism
Explain how it works in practical, first-principles terms.
## Tools and affordances
What tools are used, and why are those tools suited to this operation?
## Key variables
What factors affect success?
Examples:
- Angle
- Pressure
- Speed
- Temperature
- Moisture
- Time
- Grain direction
- Soil type
- Material hardness
- Viscosity
- Sharpness
- Surface texture
- Clearance
- Tolerance
## Good execution
What does good execution look like?
## Common mistakes
What goes wrong?
## Related operations
Compare similar or opposite processes.
## Cross-domain connections
Where does this same pattern show up elsewhere?
## Sources

Affordance analysis requirements

For every tool, object, and form note, include an ## Affordances section.

For tools, include a table like this:

| Feature | Affordance | Why it matters | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pointed blade | Penetrates soil | Concentrates force at a smaller contact area | Less effective for scraping flat surfaces |
| Curved blade | Scoops loose material | Holds soil, gravel, mulch, or compost | Can be awkward for precise edging |
| Long handle | Leverage and reach | Reduces bending and increases mechanical advantage | Less control in tight spaces |

For design/form terms, include a table like this:

| Form feature | Affordance | Why it matters | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat angled edge | Softens a sharp corner | Reduces fragility and improves touch safety | Less rounded/comfortable than a radius |
| Planar face | Creates crisp light/shadow line | Useful in furniture, architecture, and hard-surface modeling | Can still chip depending on material |

Important distinctions to capture

Be careful to distinguish similar terms.

Examples:

* Chamfer vs bevel vs fillet vs radius vs bullnose vs eased edge
* Gambrel roof vs mansard roof
* Mullion vs muntin
* Round-point shovel vs square-point shovel vs spade vs trenching shovel
* Chef knife vs utility knife vs paring knife vs serrated knife
* Blanching vs boiling vs parboiling vs steaming
* Reduction vs dilution vs concentration
* Swale vs ditch vs trench vs French drain
* Hardscape vs softscape
* Joint vs fastener vs connector
* Finish vs coating vs surface treatment
* Mesh edge vs real-world physical edge

Cross-domain linking behavior

Actively link specific terms to broader principles.

Examples:

* Chamfer should link to [[Modify Edge]], [[Remove Material]], [[Edge Treatments]], [[Surface Transitions]], and [[3D Modeling Geometry]].
* Round-Point Shovel should link to [[Penetrate Material]], [[Scoop and Carry]], [[Apply Leverage]], [[Digging]], and [[Digging and Earthworking]].
* French Drain should link to [[Control Water]], [[Control Flow]], [[Layer]], [[Drainage and Landscape Form]], and [[Grading]].
* Reduction should link to [[Concentrate]], [[Control Heat]], [[Evaporation]], and [[Cooking Techniques]] if that note exists or is created.
* Blanching should link to [[Control Heat]], [[Cooking Techniques]], [[Thermal Processing]], and [[Color and Texture Preservation]] if created.
* Chef Knife should link to [[Cut]], [[Slice]], [[Chop]], [[Edge Geometry]], [[Leverage]], and [[Ergonomics]].

Progressive understanding

Write notes so they can be understood at three levels:

1. Beginner: what it is and how to recognize it.
2. Practical: what it is used for and why.
3. Conceptual: what broader principle it illustrates.

For example, for Chamfer:

* Beginner: A chamfer is a flat angled cut across a sharp edge.
* Practical: It makes an edge less sharp, less fragile, and visually cleaner.
* Conceptual: It is an edge-modification strategy that changes the transition between two surfaces.

For Round-Point Shovel:

* Beginner: A digging shovel with a curved blade and pointed tip.
* Practical: Good for penetrating soil and scooping loose material.
* Conceptual: It combines force concentration, leverage, and containment.

Source and quality requirements

Use reliable sources where possible, such as:

* trade glossaries
* manufacturer documentation
* university extension resources
* building science references
* woodworking references
* architecture references
* landscaping references
* cooking references
* official software documentation for 3D modeling terms
* reputable textbooks or manuals

Do not copy long passages from sources. Summarize in original language.

If a term has regional, trade-specific, or ambiguous usage, say so clearly.

Final deliverables

At the end, provide a summary note or report with:

1. Folder created.
2. Number of notes created.

Important behavior

* Do not overwrite existing user notes without preserving original content.
* Do not create duplicate notes when an equivalent note already exists.
* Use clear markdown.
* Use Obsidian wikilinks.
* Prefer practical understanding over dictionary-style definitions.
* Always include affordances for tools, objects, forms, and design features.
* Always include tradeoffs where relevant.
* Always include cross-domain connections where meaningful.
* Keep notes atomic but richly linked.
* Make the system useful for real-world observation, design, repair, 3D modeling, and practical learning.

I would especially keep this sentence near the top of the task:
> The system should not merely answer “what is this called?” It should answer “what is this called, what does it let me do, why does it work that way, when is it useful, when is it not useful, and what broader principle does it reveal?”
That sentence captures the whole shift.

delegationPacket.inputContext:
Parent task: Begin sitcom world (task_1edfdc7c-d954-4108-8e21-ae057c0d1c1d)
Parent status: In Progress
Parent summary: Please start working this project, pasted response from another chat:

Yes. What you’re describing is possible, and the closest existing concept is usually called a generative-agent simulation, AI NPC sandbox, or agent-based story simulation. The important caveat is that the best version is not “AI magically generates a perfect sitcom episode from nothing.” It is a structured simulation with AI-driven characters plus a light “showrunner” system that nudges the world toward conflict, escalation, and payoff.

A useful reference point is the “Generative Agents” research project from Stanford/Google, where a small Sims-like town was populated with 25 agents who had memories, plans, reflections, relationships, and emergent social behavior. The agents could remember prior events, form plans, initiate conversations, and coordinate around events like a party. The paper specifically describes memory, reflection, planning, and retrieval as key parts of making the agents believable.   There is also an accompanying open-source repo that contains the simulation module and game environment, although it is more of a research prototype than a Unity-ready sitcom engine.  

The way I’d think about it is this:

You are not building “a sitcom” first. You are building a tiny living world where sitcom-like situations can happen.

The basic architecture

You would want several layers working together.

1. The world layer

This is Unity, Unreal, Godot, or another game engine. Unity is probably the easiest practical choice if you want lots of tutorials, NPC tools, pathfinding, animation, cameras, and asset-store support. Blender would mostly be for making or modifying sets, props, and character models. Blender is not where I’d run the simulation.

The world layer handles boring-but-crucial things: rooms, doors, chairs, counters, food, phones, pathfinding, animation, collision, time of day, and where each person is standing.

For a first version, do not start with a whole town. Start with one sitcom set: an apartment, a diner, a store, an office, or a bar. A “bottle episode” is much easier than a town simulation.

2. The character state layer

Each character needs more than a 3D model. They need a data file, almost like a character bible:

{
  "id": "mara",
  "name": "Mara Bell",
  "role": "overconfident diner owner",
  "personality": {
    "status_seeking": 0.8,
    "kindness": 0.55,
    "impulsiveness": 0.75,
    "patience": 0.25
  },
  "comedic_style": "tries to sound wise, accidentally reveals she has no idea what she is doing",
  "current_goals": [
    "prove the new espresso machine was a good purchase",
    "avoid admitting she lost the health inspection form"
  ],
  "secrets": [
    "she has been using the espresso machine manual as a coaster"
  ],
  "relationships": {
    "leo": {
      "trust": 45,
      "annoyance": 70,
      "romantic_tension": 20,
      "history": "Leo once fixed the fridge and has mentioned it every day since."
    }
  }
}

This is where the “personality” lives. Not in the model. The model is just the body. The agent data is the mind.

3. The AI decision layer

This is where an LLM or smaller AI system decides what a character wants to do next. But you should not let the AI control everything freely. The AI should choose from allowed actions.

For example, every few seconds, a character receives:

You are Mara.
Current location: diner kitchen.
You see: Leo, broken espresso machine, health inspector clipboard.
Current episode premise: the diner is trying to pass inspection during a surprise mayoral visit.
Your active goal: hide the missing inspection form.
Relevant memory: Leo promised not to tell anyone about the freezer incident, then told everyone.
Allowed actions:
- speak(target, line, emotion)
- go_to(location)
- use_object(object, action)
- start_argument(target, topic)
- lie(target, claim)
- confess(target, secret)
- wait(seconds)

Then the AI returns structured output:

{
  "action": "speak",
  "target": "Leo",
  "line": "Leo, if anyone asks, the clipboard is decorative. Very European.",
  "emotion": "forced confidence",
  "new_private_intent": "keep Leo from checking the kitchen drawer"
}

This is important: the model does not directly move the character, teleport them, or rewrite the universe. It proposes an action. The simulation checks whether the action is valid, then Unity executes it.

OpenAI-style tool/function calling is useful for this because you can define valid actions as structured functions and have the model request actions that your game code then executes. The official OpenAI docs describe this as a multi-step process where the model requests a tool call, your application executes code, then sends the result back to the model.  

4. The memory layer

This is what prevents characters from feeling like goldfish.

Each character should store memories like:

{
  "time": "episode_03_minute_05",
  "event": "Leo blamed Mara for the espresso explosion in front of the mayor.",
  "emotional_weight": 8,
  "people_involved": ["mara", "leo", "mayor"],
  "tags": ["betrayal", "public_embarrassment", "espresso_machine"]
}

Before the character makes a decision, you retrieve relevant memories. That way Mara’s next line to Leo is affected by their history. The Generative Agents paper used this general pattern: agents stored experiences in natural language, synthesized them into reflections, and retrieved relevant memories for planning.  

5. The director/showrunner layer

This is the part I would absolutely include.

A purely emergent simulation can be fascinating, but often it will be boring. Characters might wander, repeat themselves, avoid conflict, or spend ten minutes making toast. Real sitcoms are not just people acting independently; they are engineered around misunderstandings, pressure, secrets, reversals, and timing.

So you add a “director” agent that does not write every line, but does guide the episode.

The director can:

Decide the episode premise.

Assign each character a private goal.

Introduce complications.

Detect when nothing funny is happening.

Escalate conflicts.

Move characters into the same room.

Create a reveal near the end.

Trim dead air from the replay.

For example, the director might create this setup:

{
  "episode_title": "The Inspection",
  "premise": "The diner must pass inspection while everyone believes the mayor is secretly reviewing it for a local award.",
  "public_goal": "Make the diner look professional.",
  "hidden_truth": "The inspector is actually the quiet customer in booth four.",
  "character_private_goals": {
    "mara": "hide the missing inspection form",
    "leo": "prove he is useful by fixing everything too aggressively",
    "june": "get revenge because Mara forgot her birthday",
    "owen": "sell homemade candles to the mayor"
  },
  "required_escalations": [
    "a prop breaks",
    "someone lies",
    "the lie implicates another character",
    "the inspector is accidentally insulted",
    "the truth comes out in public"
  ]
}

That gives you emergence, but not chaos. It is closer to improv with a referee.

What engine/tools would make sense?

For the runtime world, I would choose Unity unless you already know Unreal. Unity has built-in and package-based support for NPC behavior systems. Unity’s own Behavior package is described as a visual tool for controlling NPCs, gameplay logic, objects, scene interactions, and cinematic orchestration.   Unity ML-Agents can also turn Unity scenes into learning environments for training agents, but I would not start there for sitcom personalities; ML-Agents is more useful for learned movement, tactics, or repeated physical behaviors than for dialogue and story.  

Unity Sentis is relevant only if you want to run trained neural networks locally inside Unity. Unity describes Sentis as a neural-network inference library that can import trained models and run them in real time on the user’s CPU or GPU.   That could matter later for local animation, perception, or smaller models, but for a first version, a cloud LLM plus ordinary Unity code is simpler.

For conversational NPC plugins, tools like Convai can speed up the “talking character” part. Convai’s Unity docs describe a Unity SDK for integrating conversational AI NPCs with dynamic dialogue and game-world behavior.   Their setup flow includes adding an API key and testing a demo scene where you can converse with an NPC.   Inworld is more focused now on real-time voice AI, TTS, speech-to-speech, and LLM routing for live conversations.   These tools are useful, but I would still build your own simulation/director layer if the goal is “episodes happen,” not just “NPCs talk.”

The MVP I would build first

The mistake would be starting with 3D models, town maps, custom animations, voice, camera work, and twenty characters. That will bury the actual experiment.

Start with a text-only prototype. It can still be a “sitcom simulator,” just not rendered yet.

Version 1 should have:

One location: a diner.

Four characters.

Ten interactable objects: counter, phone, espresso machine, clipboard, fridge, cash register, front door, table, mop, birthday cake.

One episode premise.

A 15-minute simulated runtime.

A transcript output.

The first successful output is not a playable game. It is something like:

[08:03] Mara enters the diner kitchen and hides the clipboard in the flour bin.
[08:05] Leo sees flour on Mara's sleeve and asks if the health inspector is "flour-based."
[08:06] June overhears and decides to tell the quiet customer in booth four that the diner has a flour bin full of secrets.
[08:09] Owen mistakes the inspector for the mayor and offers him a candle called "Municipal Vanilla."

If that transcript is funny or interesting, then you have the core. If it is boring, 3D graphics will not save it.

Then move to Unity

Once the text simulation works, create a graybox Unity scene.

Use capsule characters or simple low-poly models. Give each character:

A NavMeshAgent for movement.

A CharacterController or Animator for basic idle/walk/talk animations.

A speech bubble UI.

A simple “look at speaker” behavior.

A small set of action animations: talk, point, sit, pick up, use object, react, walk away.

The AI should still only choose high-level actions. Unity handles the actual performance.

For example:

public enum SimActionType
{
    Speak,
    GoTo,
    UseObject,
    React,
    Wait,
    StartArgument,
    RevealSecret
}
public class SimAction
{
    public SimActionType Type;
    public string ActorId;
    public string TargetId;
    public string LocationId;
    public string ObjectId;
    public string Dialogue;
    public string Emotion;
}

The LLM produces SimAction. Unity executes it.

The “episode runner” loop

A simple version would look like this:

1. User chooses premise:
   "The diner is being inspected, but everyone thinks the mayor is coming."
2. Showrunner generates:
   - episode title
   - initial world state
   - private goals
   - secrets
   - likely conflicts
   - escalation schedule
3. Simulation starts.
4. Every few seconds:
   - update world time
   - check character needs and goals
   - gather observations for each active character
   - retrieve relevant memories
   - ask character agent for next action
   - validate the action
   - execute action in Unity
   - store memories
   - update relationships
5. Every minute or so:
   - showrunner evaluates whether the episode is moving
   - if boring, inject a complication
   - if too chaotic, focus the scene
   - if near ending, push toward reveal/payoff
6. At the end:
   - save transcript
   - save event log
   - optionally replay with cameras
   - optionally have an editor model cut it into scenes

That gives you something you can actually run repeatedly. You change variables, press play, and a different episode happens.

What makes it feel like a sitcom instead of a chatbot room?

You need a few special ingredients.

First, every character needs wants. Sitcom characters are not just personalities; they are engines of bad decisions. One wants respect. One wants control. One wants to avoid embarrassment. One wants attention. One wants to be right. Those goals should collide.

Second, each episode needs private information. Sitcom plots thrive on secrets, misunderstandings, and mistaken assumptions.

Third, the world needs props with consequences. A broken espresso machine, a missing form, a cake, a dog, a locked bathroom, a fake award, a phone message. Props make situations concrete.

Fourth, you need relationship memory. The funny line is often funny because of who says it to whom.

Fifth, you need escalation rules. A sitcom scene should not stay flat. Every few minutes, something should get worse.

Sixth, you need editing. A raw simulation will contain pauses, wandering, repeated lines, and mundane actions. A good version records everything, then cuts together the interesting parts.

A good first project scope

I would build this in phases:

Phase 1: Text-only sitcom simulator

Four characters, one location, no Unity. The output is a transcript and event log. This proves whether the personalities, memory, conflict, and director system are fun.

Phase 2: Unity graybox

One set, simple characters, speech bubbles, basic movement. No voice yet. No fancy camera. The goal is to watch characters move around and talk.

Phase 3: Character memory and relationships

Add persistent memories across episodes. Now episode 5 can reference what happened in episode 2.

Phase 4: Director and episode structure

Add act breaks, escalation, reveals, tags, and running jokes.

Phase 5: Performance layer

Add animations, camera cuts, voice, lip sync, reactions, music stings, and timing.

Phase 6: Small-town expansion

Only after the single-set version works, add a second location, then a third. A whole small town is possible, but you want the “sitcom engine” to work before expanding the map.

The biggest technical problems

The first problem is coherence. LLM characters may forget facts, contradict the world, or invent impossible actions. You fix this by giving them strict action schemas and validating every action against the world state.

The second problem is cost and latency. You cannot call a large model for every character every frame. You call it only when a character needs a meaningful decision. Ordinary simulation code handles everything between decisions.

The third problem is boring emergence. Real people do not automatically generate funny TV. You need a director system that adds pressure.

The fourth problem is too much dialogue. AI characters love to talk. Give them physical goals and props, not just conversation prompts.

The fifth problem is tone drift. Characters may slowly stop sounding distinct. You solve that with tight character bibles, examples of their voice, and recurring behavioral rules.

My recommended build stack

For a serious but manageable prototype:

Use Unity for the world.

Use Blender only for assets, sets, and props.

Use simple premade characters at first.

Use Unity NavMesh for movement.

Use Unity Behavior, behavior trees, or simple C# state machines for baseline NPC behavior.

Use an LLM API for character decisions and dialogue.

Use structured tool/function calls for actions.

Use SQLite or a simple database for memories, relationships, and episode logs.

Use text bubbles before voice.

Add TTS and lip sync later.

Do not use ML-Agents at the beginning unless you specifically want trained physical behavior. For personality, conversation, and sitcom choices, LLM-driven agents plus hand-coded simulation rules are the better starting point.

The simplest version of the dream

You open your app and enter:

Episode premise:
The coffee shop is trying to impress a food critic, but nobody knows which customer is the critic.
Episode variables:
- Mara is hiding that the kitchen sink is broken.
- Leo thinks the critic is the man with the red scarf.
- June wants the shop to fail because she was denied a raise.
- Owen is filming a local documentary and keeps ruining private conversations.

Then you press Run Episode.

The system initializes goals and secrets, starts the simulation, characters walk around the set, talk, misunderstand each other, use props, react to surprises, and the director nudges events toward an ending. At the end, you get:

A watchable replay.

A transcript.

A list of best moments.

A saved memory state for the next episode.

That is very achievable as a prototype. The full dream — a polished, watchable, endlessly running AI sitcom with voice, camera work, timing, animation, and genuinely funny writing — is a much bigger project, but the core version is absolutely buildable if you start small and treat it as a simulation plus story-director system rather than a fully uncontrolled AI free-for-all.

Yes. What you’re describing is possible, and the closest existing concept is usually called a generative-agent simulation, AI NPC sandbox, or agent-based story simulation. The important caveat is that the best version is not “AI magically generates a perfect sitcom episode from nothing.” It is a structured simulation with AI-driven characters plus a light “showrunner” system that nudges the world toward conflict, escalation, and payoff.

A useful reference point is the “Generative Agents” research project from Stanford/Google, where a small Sims-like town was populated with 25 agents who had memories, plans, reflections, relationships, and emergent social behavior. The agents could remember prior events, form plans, initiate conversations, and coordinate around events like a party. The paper specifically describes memory, reflection, planning, and retrieval as key parts of making the agents believable.   There is also an accompanying open-source repo that contains the simulation module and game environment, although it is more of a research prototype than a Unity-ready sitcom engine.  

The way I’d think about it is this:

You are not building “a sitcom” first. You are building a tiny living world where sitcom-like situations can happen.

The basic architecture

You would want several layers working together.

1. The world layer

This is Unity, Unreal, Godot, or another game engine. Unity is probably the easiest practical choice if you want lots of tutorials, NPC tools, pathfinding, animation, cameras, and asset-store support. Blender would mostly be for making or modifying sets, props, and character models. Blender is not where I’d run the simulation.

The world layer handles boring-but-crucial things: rooms, doors, chairs, counters, food, phones, pathfinding, animation, collision, time of day, and where each person is standing.

For a first version, do not start with a whole town. Start with one sitcom set: an apartment, a diner, a store, an office, or a bar. A “bottle episode” is much easier than a town simulation.

2. The character state layer

Each character needs more than a 3D model. They need a data file, almost like a character bible:

{
  "id": "mara",
  "name": "Mara Bell",
  "role": "overconfident diner owner",
  "personality": {
    "status_seeking": 0.8,
    "kindness": 0.55,
    "impulsiveness": 0.75,
    "patience": 0.25
  },
  "comedic_style": "tries to sound wise, accidentally reveals she has no idea what she is doing",
  "current_goals": [
    "prove the new espresso machine was a good purchase",
    "avoid admitting she lost the health inspection form"
  ],
  "secrets": [
    "she has been using the espresso machine manual as a coaster"
  ],
  "relationships": {
    "leo": {
      "trust": 45,
      "annoyance": 70,
      "romantic_tension": 20,
      "history": "Leo once fixed the fridge and has mentioned it every day since."
    }
  }
}

This is where the “personality” lives. Not in the model. The model is just the body. The agent data is the mind.

3. The AI decision layer

This is where an LLM or smaller AI system decides what a character wants to do next. But you should not let the AI control everything freely. The AI should choose from allowed actions.

For example, every few seconds, a character receives:

You are Mara.
Current location: diner kitchen.
You see: Leo, broken espresso machine, health inspector clipboard.
Current episode premise: the diner is trying to pass inspection during a surprise mayoral visit.
Your active goal: hide the missing inspection form.
Relevant memory: Leo promised not to tell anyone about the freezer incident, then told everyone.
Allowed actions:
- speak(target, line, emotion)
- go_to(location)
- use_object(object, action)
- start_argument(target, topic)
- lie(target, claim)
- confess(target, secret)
- wait(seconds)

Then the AI returns structured output:

{
  "action": "speak",
  "target": "Leo",
  "line": "Leo, if anyone asks, the clipboard is decorative. Very European.",
  "emotion": "forced confidence",
  "new_private_intent": "keep Leo from checking the kitchen drawer"
}

This is important: the model does not directly move the character, teleport them, or rewrite the universe. It proposes an action. The simulation checks whether the action is valid, then Unity executes it.

OpenAI-style tool/function calling is useful for this because you can define valid actions as structured functions and have the model request actions that your game code then executes. The official OpenAI docs describe this as a multi-step process where the model requests a tool call, your application executes code, then sends the result back to the model.  

4. The memory layer

This is what prevents characters from feeling like goldfish.

Each character should store memories like:

{
  "time": "episode_03_minute_05",
  "event": "Leo blamed Mara for the espresso explosion in front of the mayor.",
  "emotional_weight": 8,
  "people_involved": ["mara", "leo", "mayor"],
  "tags": ["betrayal", "public_embarrassment", "espresso_machine"]
}

Before the character makes a decision, you retrieve relevant memories. That way Mara’s next line to Leo is affected by their history. The Generative Agents paper used this general pattern: agents stored experiences in natural language, synthesized them into reflections, and retrieved relevant memories for planning.

This is the part I would absolutely include.

A purely emergent simulation can be fascinating, but often it will be boring. Characters might wander, repeat themselves, avoid conflict, or spend ten minutes making toast. Real sitcoms are not just people acting independently; they are engineered around misunderstandings, pressure, secrets, reversals, and timing.

So you add a “director” agent that does not write every line, but does guide the episode.

The director can:

Decide the episode premise.

Assign each character a private goal.

Introduce complications.

Detect when nothing funny is happening.

Escalate conflicts.

Move characters into the same room.

Create a reveal near the end.

{

  "premise": "The diner must pass inspection while everyone believes the mayor is secretly reviewing it for a local award.",
  "public_goal": "Make the diner look professional.",
  "hidden_truth": "The inspector is actually the quiet customer in booth four.",
  "character_private_goals": {
    "mara": "hide the missing inspection form",
    "leo": "prove he is useful by fixing everything too aggressively",
    "june": "get revenge because Mara forgot her birthday",
    "owen": "sell homemade candles to the mayor"
  },
  "required_escalations": [

    "someone lies",
    "the lie implicates another character",
    "the inspector is accidentally insulted",

  "new_private_intent": "keep Leo from checking the kitchen drawer"
}

This is important: the model does not directly move the character, teleport them, or rewrite the universe. It proposes an action. The simulation checks whether the action is valid, then Unity executes it.

OpenAI-style tool/function calling is useful for this because you can define valid actions as structured functions and have the model request actions that your game code then executes. The official OpenAI docs describe this as a multi-step process where the model requests a tool call, your application executes code, then sends the result back to the model.  

4. The memory layer

This is what prevents characters from feeling like goldfish.

Each character should store memories like:

{
  "time": "episode_03_minute_05",
  "event": "Leo blamed Mara for the espresso explosion in front of the mayor.",
  "emotional_weight": 8,
  "people_involved": ["mara", "leo", "mayor"],
  "tags": ["betrayal", "public_embarrassment", "espresso_machine"]
}

Before the character makes a decision, you retrieve relevant memories. That way Mara’s next line to Leo is affected by their history. The Generative Agents paper used this general pattern: agents stored experiences in natural language, synthesized them into reflections, and retrieved relevant memories for planning.  

5. The director/showrunner layer

This is the part I would absolutely include.

A purely emergent simulation can be fascinating, but often it will be boring. Characters might wander, repeat themselves, avoid conflict, or spend ten minutes making toast. Real sitcoms are not just people acting independently; they are engineered around misunderstandings, pressure, secrets, reversals, and timing.

So you add a “director” agent that does not write every line, but does guide the episode.

The director can:

Decide the episode premise.

Assign each character a private goal.

Introduce complications.

Detect when nothing funny is happening.

Escalate conflicts.

Move characters into the same room.

Create a reveal near the end.

Trim dead air from the replay.

For example, the director might create this setup:

{
  "episode_title": "The Inspection",
  "premise": "The diner must pass inspection while everyone believes the mayor is secretly reviewing it for a local award.",
  "public_goal": "Make the diner look professional.",
  "hidden_truth": "The inspector is actually the quiet customer in booth four.",
  "character_private_goals": {
    "mara": "hide the missing inspection form",
    "leo": "prove he is useful by fixing everything too aggressively",
    "june": "get revenge because Mara forgot her birthday",
    "owen": "sell homemade candles to the mayor"
  },
  "required_escalations": [
    "a prop breaks",
    "someone lies",
    "the lie implicates another character",
    "the inspector is accidentally insulted",
    "the truth comes out in public"
  ]
}

That gives you emergence, but not chaos. It is closer to improv with a referee.

What engine/tools would make sense?

For the runtime world, I would choose Unity unless you already know Unreal. Unity has built-in and package-based support for NPC behavior systems. Unity’s own Behavior package is described as a visual tool for controlling NPCs, gameplay logic, objects, scene interactions, and cinematic orchestration.   Unity ML-Agents can also turn Unity scenes into learning environments for training agents, but I would not start there for sitcom personalities; ML-Agents is more useful for learned movement, tactics, or repeated physical behaviors than for dialogue and story.  

Unity Sentis is relevant only if you want to run trained neural networks locally inside Unity. Unity describes Sentis as a neural-network inference library that can import trained models and run them in real time on the user’s CPU or GPU.   That could matter later for local animation, perception, or smaller models, but for a first version, a cloud LLM plus ordinary Unity code is simpler.

For conversational NPC plugins, tools like Convai can speed up the “talking character” part. Convai’s Unity docs describe a Unity SDK for integrating conversational AI NPCs with dynamic dialogue and game-world behavior.   Their setup flow includes adding an API key and testing a demo scene where you can converse with an NPC.   Inworld is more focused now on real-time voice AI, TTS, speech-to-speech, and LLM routing for live conversations.   These tools are useful, but I would still build your own simulation/director layer if the goal is “episodes happen,” not just “NPCs talk.”

The MVP I would build first

The mistake would be starting with 3D models, town maps, custom animations, voice, camera work, and twenty characters. That will bury the actual experiment.

Start with a text-only prototype. It can still be a “sitcom simulator,” just not rendered yet.

Version 1 should have:

One location: a diner.

Four characters.

Ten interactable objects: counter, phone, espresso machine, clipboard, fridge, cash register, front door, table, mop, birthday cake.

One episode premise.

A 15-minute simulated runtime.

A transcript output.

The first successful output is not a playable game. It is something like:

[08:03] Mara enters the diner kitchen and hides the clipboard in the flour bin.
[08:05] Leo sees flour on Mara's sleeve and asks if the health inspector is "flour-based."
[08:06] June overhears and decides to tell the quiet customer in booth four that the diner has a flour bin full of secrets.
[08:09] Owen mistakes the inspector for the mayor and offers him a candle called "Municipal Vanilla."

If that transcript is funny or interesting, then you have the core. If it is boring, 3D graphics will not save it.

Then move to Unity

Once the text simulation works, create a graybox Unity scene.

Use capsule characters or simple low-poly models. Give each character:

A NavMeshAgent for movement.

A CharacterController or Animator for basic idle/walk/talk animations.

A speech bubble UI.

A simple “look at speaker” behavior.

A small set of action animations: talk, point, sit, pick up, use object, react, walk away.

The AI should still only choose high-level actions. Unity handles the actual performance.

For example:

public enum SimActionType
{
    Speak,
    GoTo,
    UseObject,
    React,
    Wait,
    StartArgument,
    RevealSecret
}
public class SimAction
{
    public SimActionType Type;
    public string ActorId;
    public string TargetId;
    public string LocationId;
    public string ObjectId;
    public string Dialogue;
    public string Emotion;
}

The LLM produces SimAction. Unity executes it.

The “episode runner” loop

A simple version would look like this:

1. User chooses premise:
   "The diner is being inspected, but everyone thinks the mayor is coming."
2. Showrunner generates:
   - episode title
   - initial world state
   - private goals
   - secrets
   - likely conflicts
   - escalation schedule
3. Simulation starts.
4. Every few seconds:
   - update world time
   - check character needs and goals
   - gather observations for each active character
   - retrieve relevant memories
   - ask character agent for next action
   - validate the action
   - execute action in Unity
   - store memories
   - update relationships
5. Every minute or so:
   - showrunner evaluates whether the episode is moving
   - if boring, inject a complication
   - if too chaotic, focus the scene
   - if near ending, push toward reveal/payoff
6. At the end:
   - save transcript
   - save event log
   - optionally replay with cameras
   - optionally have an editor model cut it into scenes

That gives you something you can actually run repeatedly. You change variables, press play, and a different episode happens.

What makes it feel like a sitcom instead of a chatbot room?

You need a few special ingredients.

First, every character needs wants. Sitcom characters are not just personalities; they are engines of bad decisions. One wants respect. One wants control. One wants to avoid embarrassment. One wants attention. One wants to be right. Those goals should collide.

Second, each episode needs private information. Sitcom plots thrive on secrets, misunderstandings, and mistaken assumptions.

Third, the world needs props with consequences. A broken espresso machine, a missing form, a cake, a dog, a locked bathroom, a fake award, a phone message. Props make situations concrete.

Fourth, you need relationship memory. The funny line is often funny because of who says it to whom.

Fifth, you need escalation rules. A sitcom scene should not stay flat. Every few minutes, something should get worse.

Sixth, you need editing. A raw simulation will contain pauses, wandering, repeated lines, and mundane actions. A good version records everything, then cuts together the interesting parts.

A good first project scope

I would build this in phases:

Phase 1: Text-only sitcom simulator

Four characters, one location, no Unity. The output is a transcript and event log. This proves whether the personalities, memory, conflict, and director system are fun.

Phase 2: Unity graybox

One set, simple characters, speech bubbles, basic movement. No voice yet. No fancy camera. The goal is to watch characters move around and talk.

Phase 3: Character memory and relationships

Add persistent memories across episodes. Now episode 5 can reference what happened in episode 2.

Phase 4: Director and episode structure

Add act breaks, escalation, reveals, tags, and running jokes.

Phase 5: Performance layer

Add animations, camera cuts, voice, lip sync, reactions, music stings, and timing.

Phase 6: Small-town expansion

Only after the single-set version works, add a second location, then a third. A whole small town is possible, but you want the “sitcom engine” to work before expanding the map.

The biggest technical problems

The first problem is coherence. LLM characters may forget facts, contradict the world, or invent impossible actions. You fix this by giving them strict action schemas and validating every action against the world state.

The second problem is cost and latency. You cannot call a large model for every character every frame. You call it only when a character needs a meaningful decision. Ordinary simulation code handles everything between decisions.

The third problem is boring emergence. Real people do not automatically generate funny TV. You need a director system that adds pressure.

The fourth problem is too much dialogue. AI characters love to talk. Give them physical goals and props, not just conversation prompts.

The fifth problem is tone drift. Characters may slowly stop sounding distinct. You solve that with tight character bibles, examples of their voice, and recurring behavioral rules.

My recommended build stack

For a serious but manageable prototype:

Use Unity for the world.

Use Blender only for assets, sets, and props.

Use simple premade characters at first.

Use Unity NavMesh for movement.

Use Unity Behavior, behavior trees, or simple C# state machines for baseline NPC behavior.

Use an LLM API for character decisions and dialogue.

Use structured tool/function calls for actions.

Use SQLite or a simple database for memories, relationships, and episode logs.

Use text bubbles before voice.

Add TTS and lip sync later.

Do not use ML-Agents at the beginning unless you specifically want trained physical behavior. For personality, conversation, and sitcom choices, LLM-driven agents plus hand-coded simulation rules are the better starting point.

The simplest version of the dream

You open your app and enter:

Episode premise:
The coffee shop is trying to impress a food critic, but nobody knows which customer is the critic.
Episode variables:
- Mara is hiding that the kitchen sink is broken.
- Leo thinks the critic is the man with the red scarf.
- June wants the shop to fail because she was denied a raise.
- Owen is filming a local documentary and keeps ruining private conversations.

Then you press Run Episode.

The system initializes goals and secrets, starts the simulation, characters walk around the set, talk, misunderstand each other, use props, react to surprises, and the director nudges events toward an ending. At the end, you get:

A watchable replay.

A transcript.

A list of best moments.

A saved memory state for the next episode.

That is very achievable as a prototype. The full dream — a polished, watchable, endlessly running AI sitcom with voice, camera work, timing, animation, and genuinely funny writing — is a much bigger project, but the core version is absolutely buildable if you start small and treat it as a simulation plus story-director system rather than a fully uncontrolled AI free-for-all.

tasks.title: Model 3920 Silver Oak St.
tasks.summary: I started modeling a property/house in Sketchup, originally to plan/plot the garden but now I'm kinda interested in modeling the whole thing in detail (over time, iteratively) for the sake of it. I've started doing some basic 2d drawing/mapping of the property. I've got the building, driveway, garden beds, patio/deck, etc. and property lines roughly drawn out. Any ideas/thoughts on how best to continue this project? What order should I do this stuff in? Please use the appropriate/relevant agent skills

tasks.title: Begin sitcom world
tasks.summary:
Please start working this project, pasted response from another chat:

Yes. What you’re describing is possible, and the closest existing concept is usually called a generative-agent simulation, AI NPC sandbox, or agent-based story simulation. The important caveat is that the best version is not “AI magically generates a perfect sitcom episode from nothing.” It is a structured simulation with AI-driven characters plus a light “showrunner” system that nudges the world toward conflict, escalation, and payoff.

A useful reference point is the “Generative Agents” research project from Stanford/Google, where a small Sims-like town was populated with 25 agents who had memories, plans, reflections, relationships, and emergent social behavior. The agents could remember prior events, form plans, initiate conversations, and coordinate around events like a party. The paper specifically describes memory, reflection, planning, and retrieval as key parts of making the agents believable.   There is also an accompanying open-source repo that contains the simulation module and game environment, although it is more of a research prototype than a Unity-ready sitcom engine.  

The way I’d think about it is this:

You are not building “a sitcom” first. You are building a tiny living world where sitcom-like situations can happen.

The basic architecture

You would want several layers working together.

1. The world layer

This is Unity, Unreal, Godot, or another game engine. Unity is probably the easiest practical choice if you want lots of tutorials, NPC tools, pathfinding, animation, cameras, and asset-store support. Blender would mostly be for making or modifying sets, props, and character models. Blender is not where I’d run the simulation.

The world layer handles boring-but-crucial things: rooms, doors, chairs, counters, food, phones, pathfinding, animation, collision, time of day, and where each person is standing.

For a first version, do not start with a whole town. Start with one sitcom set: an apartment, a diner, a store, an office, or a bar. A “bottle episode” is much easier than a town simulation.

2. The character state layer

Each character needs more than a 3D model. They need a data file, almost like a character bible:

{
  "id": "mara",
  "name": "Mara Bell",
  "role": "overconfident diner owner",
  "personality": {
    "status_seeking": 0.8,
    "kindness": 0.55,
    "impulsiveness": 0.75,
    "patience": 0.25
  },
  "comedic_style": "tries to sound wise, accidentally reveals she has no idea what she is doing",
  "current_goals": [
    "prove the new espresso machine was a good purchase",
    "avoid admitting she lost the health inspection form"
  ],
  "secrets": [
    "she has been using the espresso machine manual as a coaster"
  ],
  "relationships": {
    "leo": {
      "trust": 45,
      "annoyance": 70,
      "romantic_tension": 20,
      "history": "Leo once fixed the fridge and has mentioned it every day since."
    }
  }
}

This is where the “personality” lives. Not in the model. The model is just the body. The agent data is the mind.

This is where an LLM or smaller AI system decides what a character wants to do next. But you should not let the AI control everything freely. The AI should choose from allowed actions.

Current location: diner kitchen.
You see: Leo, broken espresso machine, health inspector clipboard.
Current episode premise: the diner is trying to pass inspection during a surprise mayoral visit.
Your active goal: hide the missing inspection form.
Relevant memory: Leo promised not to tell anyone about the freezer incident, then told everyone.
Allowed actions:
- speak(target, line, emotion)
- go_to(location)
- use_object(object, action)
- start_argument(target, topic)
- lie(target, claim)
- confess(target, secret)
- wait(seconds)

Then the AI returns structured output:

{
  "action": "speak",
  "target": "Leo",
  "line": "Leo, if anyone asks, the clipboard is decorative. Very European.",
  "emotion": "forced confidence",
  "new_private_intent": "keep Leo from checking the kitchen drawer"
}

This is what prevents characters from feeling like goldfish.

tasks.title: Begin emergence project
tasks.summary:
You are working in a new local project folder:

/Users/colinfreed/projects/experiments/emergence

The project name is Emergence. The Python package name should be `emergence`.

Build the first working version of a Mesa-based artificial ecology / agent-based survival simulation. This is Phase 1 only. Do not try to build the whole long-term ontology/fire/culture system yet. The goal is to create a clean, extensible foundation that we can grow in later phases.

Conceptual goal:
Emergence is a simulation framework for studying agents embedded inside a rule-governed world. The first version should model a simple survival ecology:

- agents exist in a grid world
- agents have energy
- agents lose energy each step
- movement costs energy
- food exists in grid cells
- eating food restores energy
- agents die when energy reaches zero
- different behavior policies can be compared
- results are logged and testable

Important clarification:
Do NOT use LLMs, Codex, or AI agents inside the simulation itself. Codex is only helping write the code. The simulated agents should initially be simple Python agents with rule-based policies.

Use modern Python and Mesa.
Prefer current Mesa 3.x style. Avoid old deprecated scheduler patterns such as RandomActivation if possible. Use Mesa’s current Model/Agent/AgentSet/DataCollector patterns where practical. If any Mesa API details are uncertain, inspect the installed package and adjust. The code must actually run.

Create a real Python project, not a single script.

Please do the following:

1. Inspect the directory first.
   - If it is empty, create the project from scratch.
   - If files already exist, preserve them unless clearly generated/empty.
   - Do not ask clarifying questions unless absolutely blocked. Make reasonable assumptions and proceed.

2. Create this approximate structure:

emergence/
  pyproject.toml
  README.md
  AGENTS.md
  configs/
    phase1_energy_world.yaml
  src/
    emergence/
      __init__.py
      actions.py
      agents.py
      cli.py
      config.py
      grid.py
      metrics.py
      model.py
      policies.py
  tests/
    test_actions.py
    test_config.py
    test_death.py
    test_eating.py
    test_energy.py
    test_model_smoke.py
    test_policies.py

It is fine to add small helper files if needed, but keep the project simple.

3. Dependencies:
Use a `pyproject.toml` with:
- Python >= 3.11
- mesa >= 3, < 4
- numpy
- pandas
- pydantic
- pyyaml
- matplotlib
- pytest as a dev/test dependency if using optional extras

Use a simple build backend such as hatchling or setuptools. Pick one and make it work.

4. Create AGENTS.md with project instructions for future coding agents.

AGENTS.md should include:
- this is a Mesa-based artificial ecology simulation
- keep world mechanics separate from agent decision policies
- keep actions separate from policies
- keep configuration separate from model logic
- add or update tests whenever mechanics change
- never let dead agents act
- never allow food quantities below zero
- energy should remain numeric and bounded where applicable
- avoid hard-coding experiment parameters in model logic
- use config files for scenario parameters
- after changes, run pytest
- prefer small, testable increments

5. Implement Phase 1 mechanics.

Core world:
- rectangular grid with width and height from config
- food amount per cell
- optional food regrowth each step
- one or more agents
- model step loop
- seeded randomness for reproducibility
- data collection through Mesa DataCollector if practical

Agent state:
- position
- energy
- age
- alive/dead status
- vision range
- food_eaten count
- policy name

Actions:
- move north
- move south
- move east
- move west
- eat
- wait

Action rules:
- every living agent pays metabolism cost per step
- moving also pays movement cost
- invalid movement should either become wait or remain in place, but behavior must be documented and tested
- eating consumes one food unit from the current cell if available
- eating increases energy by food_energy, capped at max_energy
- food quantity cannot go below zero
- if energy <= 0, the agent becomes dead
- dead agents do not act

Policies:
Implement at least:
- RandomPolicy: chooses randomly among valid primitive actions
- GreedyVisibleFoodPolicy: if food is visible within vision range, moves one step toward a visible food cell; if standing on food, eats; otherwise falls back to random movement or wait
- MemoryFoodPolicy: remembers cells where food was previously seen or eaten and tends to move toward remembered food locations; keep it simple

Policy interface:
- each policy should expose something like `choose_action(agent, observation) -> Action`
- the model/agent should not care which policy is being used

Observation:
Create a simple observation object or dict that gives an agent:
- its position
- current energy
- visible food cells within vision range
- current cell food amount
- nearby grid bounds info if useful

6. Config:
Use Pydantic or dataclasses to define a configuration object.

The YAML config should include:
- width
- height
- initial_agents
- initial_food_cells
- initial_food_per_cell
- food_regrowth_probability
- max_food_per_cell
- initial_energy
- max_energy
- metabolism_cost
- movement_cost
- food_energy
- vision_range
- max_steps
- default_policy
- seed

Provide a default config file at:

configs/phase1_energy_world.yaml

7. CLI:
Create a simple CLI so I can run:

python -m emergence.cli run --config configs/phase1_energy_world.yaml

The CLI should:
- load config
- run the model for max_steps or until all agents are dead
- print a concise summary:
  - steps run
  - alive agents
  - dead agents
  - total food eaten
  - mean final energy
  - total food remaining
- optionally write outputs to an `outputs/` folder if easy, but do not overcomplicate

8. Metrics:
Create reusable metric functions:
- count_alive_agents
- count_dead_agents
- total_food
- total_food_eaten
- mean_energy
- maybe mean_age

Use these in the model DataCollector if practical.

9. Tests:
Write pytest tests for the important invariants.

Minimum tests:
- config loads from YAML
- model initializes with expected number of agents
- moving changes position when valid
- moving costs energy
- waiting costs metabolism
- eating increases energy and decreases food
- food cannot go below zero
- agent dies when energy reaches zero or below
- dead agent cannot act
- RandomPolicy returns a valid action
- GreedyVisibleFoodPolicy eats when standing on food
- GreedyVisibleFoodPolicy moves toward visible food
- smoke test: run 10 steps with no exceptions

Tests should be deterministic where possible by using seeds.

10. README:
Create a README.md that explains:
- what emergence is
- Phase 1 scope
- how to install
- how to run tests
- how to run the first simulation
- current architecture
- future phases:
  - Phase 2: memory and exploration experiments
  - Phase 3: competition and resource scarcity
  - Phase 4: reproduction and mutation
  - Phase 5: object affordances
  - Phase 6: ontology/belief layer
  - Phase 7: discovery, communication, and culture

11. Verification:
After implementing, run:
- python -m pytest -q
- python -m emergence.cli run --config configs/phase1_energy_world.yaml

Fix any errors until both pass.

12. Final response:
When done, summarize:
- files created
- how to install/run
- test results
- what the first simulation currently does
- recommended next implementation step

Keep the implementation clean and minimal. Do not over-engineer. The first milestone is a working, tested Energy World: agents can move, eat, lose energy, die, and be compared by simple policies.

tasks.title: AWMS Conditions and Symptoms
tasks.summary:
All right, so something else that needs to be figured out for the animal welfare monitoring system is how to handle conditions and illnesses.

If you’re trying to predict potential issues, you’re essentially trying to predict unstable states of the animal. So what does that mean? You need representations for that—identifying common categories of deviations from a stable state. Things like mastitis, heat stress, etc.

So the question becomes: what is a “condition”? What defines it? Is mastitis a condition? Is heat stress a condition? And then what are indicators—are those separate entities? For example, body temperature is a measurement, and an indicator would be when it falls outside a certain range.

So I’m trying to figure out how to structure this—how to design and architect it in the system. I don’t want to hard-code everything, especially since these things aren’t perfectly discrete. Most conditions exist on a spectrum—they’re not just a binary switch where something is definitively true or not.

Instead, it’s more about probabilities—an animal exhibiting patterns that are highly correlated with a condition. So the system needs to handle that kind of uncertainty.

For example, mastitis is a condition with symptoms and causes. So symptoms would be identifiable through data. The question is: what are the symptoms, and how are they represented? What data corresponds to those symptoms?

Then you have to consider how the system processes incoming sensor data—mapping it to animals, comparing it against baselines, detecting deviations, and determining whether those deviations match known symptom patterns.

From there, you’d evaluate how strongly those symptoms correlate with specific conditions. That could lead to predictions about potential health issues.

So the challenge is how to represent all of this—conditions, symptoms, indicators, measurements—in a flexible way. It should allow updates and modifications as new information becomes available, rather than relying on hard-coded definitions.

For example, instead of hard-coding mastitis as a fixed condition, it should be something that can be defined and updated within the system.

So the key questions are: what are the core entities—conditions, symptoms, indicators—and how do they relate? And how do you design the system so it’s extensible and scalable, not just for dairy cows but for other animals as well?

That’s what I’m trying to think through—how to design it so it’s flexible and not overly rigid.

tasks.title: Improve mathematics knowledge
tasks.summary:
All right, so I need you to help me improve my knowledge and understanding of mathematics.

A little background: I’m not completely unfamiliar with math, but it’s never been one of my strong points. A lot of that is because of how abstract it can be. For me, it’s easier to understand things when I see them in action or work through them. I’m not the type of person who can just hear rules, memorize them, and then apply them—I need to actually understand what’s going on.

So what I’m trying to do is refresh my understanding of math—basically do some remedial learning—and then build up from there. I’m not trying to go infinitely deep, but I want to understand it well enough to communicate mathematically and across fields like physics, biology, and computer science.

I also want it to support things like systems design, understanding complex systems, optimization, and engineering. Right now, when I look at math-heavy content, it often feels like a foreign language, so I want to actually understand it instead of just skimming and being confused.

Ideally, this will help with the projects I’m working on. I want to go through and expand any notes I have on math and work through the fundamentals again. I made it up to Calc 3 in college, but I did poorly in those classes. In grade school and high school, I did well, but it didn’t feel like I truly understood the material—I was more just figuring out how to get good grades.

So I’ve been exposed to arithmetic, geometry, algebra, and trigonometry, but I’m pretty fuzzy on a lot of it since I haven’t used it much. Again, practical use cases help me understand things much better than purely theoretical examples.

So I’d like to start by refining and expanding fundamental notes and defining some kind of learning path—going through the basics to solidify my understanding, then moving into more advanced topics. Ideally, the focus would be on math that’s actually applicable to what I’m working on.

It would help to structure this as a kind of curriculum—starting with fundamentals, moving into intermediate topics, and then branching into more advanced areas based on relevance.

I also want to integrate this into my Obsidian vault—adding notes and creating a system for reviewing and expanding my knowledge over time.

tasks.title: Improve task detail page UI/UX
tasks.summary:
On the task detail page, I don’t understand what the “agent used,” “agent
  guidance,” “current context,” or “recommendations” sections are for. They take
  up a lot of space at the top of the page, and I don’t even know why they’re
  there. At minimum, those sections should be moved to the bottom.

  More broadly, the page feels too busy. There’s too much happening at once,
  especially for the user’s likely intent. If I open a task, I usually want to
  edit the task brief or instructions right away, but it’s difficult to quickly
  find and get to that section. That’s a poor user experience. The task name,
  brief, and instructions need to be much easier to access.

  The execution settings section also needs work. The form fields are hard to
  read because the layout doesn’t give the content enough space. The width and
  overall structure should be adjusted so the fields are easier to scan and
  understand.

  There are also several places where too much content is shown at once, which
  makes the layout feel overwhelming and stretches sections unnecessarily. For
  example, the requested prompt skills section shows too many tags at once,
  making it overly long and taking up too much real estate. That makes it harder
  to scroll to and navigate to the content that matters more often.

  The routing and governance section is one I use frequently, usually to change
  the required sandbox field or choose the role. Right below that, the
  dependencies section has a similar problem: it appears to show every task at
  once for dependency selection, which is not a good UX/UI pattern.

  Those are the main issues I want to call out on the task detail page. They’re
  all UI/UX problems that should be addressed.

  read because the layout doesn’t give the content enough space. The width and
  overall structure should be adjusted so the fields are easier to scan and
  understand.

  There are also several places where too much content is shown at once, which
  makes the layout feel overwhelming and stretches sections unnecessarily. For
  example, the requested prompt skills section shows too many tags at once,
  making it overly long and taking up too much real estate. That makes it harder
  to scroll to and navigate to the content that matters more often.

  The routing and governance section is one I use frequently, usually to change
  the required sandbox field or choose the role. Right below that, the
  dependencies section has a similar problem: it appears to show every task at
  once for dependency selection, which is not a good UX/UI pattern.

  Those are the main issues I want to call out on the task detail page. They’re
  all UI/UX problems that should be addressed.

delegationPacket.doneCondition: Finish the Current-State UI/UX Assessment step for Improve task detail page UI/UX.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Report completion back to parent task Improve task detail page UI/UX.
