tasks.title: Improve workflows UI/UX
tasks.summary:
Yeah, so this is about the workflow UI/UX in the agent management system, which definitely needs improvement.

If you go to the workflows page, everything is kind of thrown together. It needs a pass on progressive disclosure and information architecture.

In general, if there are multiple records of an entity, the pattern should be: show a list of records, make them searchable, and allow users to drill into a specific instance by clicking a row or preview component. That preview should show high-level, relevant information about the entity, and then open a detailed view when selected. That’s a standard and effective structure for UX and information architecture.

Applied here, the workflows page should show existing workflows in a list or table format, with high-level, relevant information only—no information overload in the preview. Then users can open a workflow to view its details.

The detail page should be designed to support editing and modifying a workflow. Similarly, creating a workflow—likely from the list page—should have a UI that supports adding tasks, sequencing them, or making them parallelizable.

This should follow the same progressive disclosure approach: don’t show everything at once. Show the most common or relevant options first, then allow users to expand into more advanced or less frequently used options as needed, ordered from most used to least used.

So this approach should be applied to the workflows page, the workflow detail view, and likely other parts of the agent management system. Address the concrete issues first, then review for additional opportunities to improve.

tasks.title: Improve task creation
tasks.summary:
All right, so getting some more thoughts and notes down on how we can improve adding tasks in the agent management system.

Right now, there’s a single “add task” button, and on the form there are two options—quick task and full task views. I don’t think I like opening a single form and switching between those as the only option. I’d rather have different entry points depending on context.

For example, a quick add task button on the tasks page that opens a simplified form. There are some issues with the current quick task form—it’s too limited. For instance, preferred role should be available there. That’s one example, but I won’t go too deep into that here.

Then, a separate full task button that opens the full form. Also, the full task form itself is still too overwhelming and poorly laid out, so that needs to be addressed.

One concrete issue is the dependency section—where you can mark other tasks as dependencies. It shows way too much at once. The preview cards for each task are too heavy. It should be more like a search-based interface that takes up less space and makes it easier to find and select tasks, possibly multiple at once.

So things like that need to be streamlined and improved for a better user experience.

In general, we need to review both the quick task form and the full task form. The full task form especially needs cleanup in layout and composition to make more sense and feel more usable.

Another consideration is presentation. The quick task form probably still makes sense as a modal, but the full task form might warrant a full-screen modal or even its own page.

Overall, this is about improving the UX/UI around adding tasks—making it more flexible and appropriate to the user’s context. It should be easy and efficient to create and modify tasks.

Given the variety of what a task can represent, we need better contextual handling—using progressive disclosure so users aren’t overwhelmed. Show the most relevant information first, prioritize what matters, and make it easy to navigate between sections.

So, address the concrete issues mentioned, then do a broader review to find other ways to streamline task creation and management.

tasks.title: Roles for 4 different types of docs
tasks.summary:
Different roles for each of the types of—I don’t know where I saw it—but someone had a framework of four different types of documentation. Basically, I’m thinking we create a role for each of those.

That way, we have an agent or role that’s specialized and highly capable at handling each of those types or formats of documentation or writing.

So yeah, figure that out and implement that.

tasks.title: Lead sourcer role
tasks.summary:
All right, so another thing I’m thinking of is needing—well, I think there might have already been a role created, but just double checking on this. Basically, a role or workflow where a lead sourcer—essentially a role that an agent or person fills, who is good at going out and finding leads.

What I mean is someone who can take the product, understand its value and the pain points it solves, and figure out what markets, industries, organizations, or people would have those problems. Then, essentially, find those people—potential users, customers, the organizations they form—and figure out where they are and how to contact them.

So, doing the research and discovery to help build a contact book, or at least identify where to go to find potential customers—people we can actually help and bring value to via our product, whether that’s Kwipoo or anything else we create.

So yeah, that’s the idea—whether it’s a role, a workflow, or both. Just capturing that

tasks.title: Add inline quick actions to tasks queue
tasks.summary: Add 1-2 high-frequency inline actions to the tasks queue so common operations do not require opening the full task. Focus on actions that are safe, frequent, and clearly valuable from the queue itself, such as opening the thread directly or advancing a task into an obvious next state. Keep the queue dense and avoid turning rows into a button farm.
tasks.successCriteria: The tasks queue exposes a small set of clearly useful inline actions, preserves scanability, and the actions work correctly for the states they are shown in.
tasks.readyCondition: The specific queue actions to expose and the state guards for each are clear enough to implement without inventing new workflow behavior.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Operators can perform the most common queue-level actions faster without leaving the tasks page.

tasks.title: Add saved task queue views and presets
tasks.summary: Add lightweight saved or preset task queue views on the tasks page so operators can quickly switch between common working contexts. Start with pragmatic defaults such as Open tasks, Needs attention, and Completed, and keep the behavior simple enough to support daily use without adding configuration overhead.
tasks.successCriteria: The tasks page offers practical preset views that meaningfully reduce manual filtering and sorting, with clear labels and predictable results.
tasks.readyCondition: The initial preset views and the filters or sorts that define them are agreed well enough to implement directly.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Operators can move between common queue contexts with one click instead of rebuilding the same view repeatedly.

tasks.title: Implement Freemium Model (Free vs Premium)
tasks.summary:
Purpose

You are responsible for designing and implementing a complete, end-to-end freemium system for Kwipoo that enables:

A free tier with enforced usage limits
A paid subscription tier with expanded limits
Reliable, consistent enforcement of restrictions
A system that is simple now but adaptable later

This is not just about adding payments—it is about building a coherent access control layer tied to account status.

Core Objective

Deliver a working system where:

Users can use the app for free with a limit of 100 Things
Users can upgrade to a paid plan (~$5/month) to unlock expanded usage (higher limits + storage)
The system correctly enforces limits based on account status
Subscription state is accurate, secure, and reflected in real time (or near real time)
The implementation is structured to support future changes (pricing, limits, tiers)
Definition of Done

This task is complete when:

Free Tier Enforcement
Free users cannot exceed 100 Things
Attempts to exceed limits are blocked cleanly with clear feedback
Paid Tier Activation
Users can successfully subscribe via a payment provider
Their account status updates to “premium”
Premium Behavior
Premium users are no longer constrained by the 100 Thing limit (within defined bounds)
Storage/usage allowances are increased appropriately
Downgrade / Failure Handling
If a subscription expires, fails, or is canceled:
The system correctly reverts the user to free-tier restrictions
Existing data is preserved, but new actions are restricted if over limit
System Integrity
Limits are enforced server-side, not just in UI
No obvious bypasses (e.g. direct API calls to create Things past the limit)
Configurability
Limits (e.g. “100 Things”) and pricing-related logic are not hardcoded in scattered places
Values can be updated centrally with minimal code changes
Guiding Principles
Enforcement must be authoritative
UI is advisory; backend is the source of truth.
Start simple, but not rigid
Avoid overengineering tiers, but don’t hardcode yourself into a dead end.
Protect against edge cases early
Subscription systems fail in weird ways—design defensively.
User experience matters at boundaries
Hitting limits should feel clear and fair, not broken or confusing.
System > feature
This is not a one-off feature; it is a foundational layer for monetization.
Key System Responsibilities

The implementation must account for:

1. Account Tier State
Free vs Premium designation
Source of truth (database field, subscription status, etc.)
2. Usage Tracking
Accurate count of Things per user
Storage usage (if applicable)
3. Enforcement Layer
Blocking creation when limits are exceeded
Applied consistently across all entry points (UI, API, background processes)
4. Billing Integration
Subscription creation and confirmation
Webhook handling (for status updates)
Syncing external billing state → internal account state
5. State Transitions
Free → Premium (upgrade)
Premium → Free (downgrade, failed payment, cancellation)
Critical Edge Cases (Must Be Considered)
User is over 100 Things, then subscription expires
→ What is blocked vs allowed?
Payment succeeds but webhook is delayed/fails
→ Temporary access inconsistency
User tries to spam create Things rapidly at the limit boundary
→ Race conditions / duplicate writes
User deletes items after hitting limit
→ Can they resume normal behavior immediately?
Offline usage (if applicable)
→ How are limits enforced when reconnecting?
Constraints
Do not introduce unnecessary complexity (no multi-tier pricing systems yet)
Do not build speculative features (teams, enterprise, etc.)
Avoid deeply coupling billing logic with unrelated systems
Keep implementation understandable and maintainable
Success Criteria
A user can:
Use the app for free up to a clear limit
Upgrade and immediately gain expanded capability
Be restricted again if their subscription ends
The system:
Cannot be trivially bypassed
Is resilient to common failure scenarios
Can be modified later without major refactoring
Failure Modes to Avoid
Only enforcing limits in the frontend
Hardcoding limits across multiple files/services
Treating billing state as always reliable/truthful
Not handling downgrade scenarios
Building an overly complex pricing abstraction too early
Notes for Execution
Favor a centralized “entitlement/limits” model rather than scattered checks
Think in terms of:
“What is this user allowed to do?”
Not just “are they premium?”

→ What is blocked vs allowed?
Payment succeeds but webhook is delayed/fails
→ Temporary access inconsistency
User tries to spam create Things rapidly at the limit boundary
→ Race conditions / duplicate writes
User deletes items after hitting limit
→ Can they resume normal behavior immediately?
Offline usage (if applicable)
→ How are limits enforced when reconnecting?
Constraints
Do not introduce unnecessary complexity (no multi-tier pricing systems yet)
Do not build speculative features (teams, enterprise, etc.)
Avoid deeply coupling billing logic with unrelated systems
Keep implementation understandable and maintainable
Success Criteria
A user can:
Use the app for free up to a clear limit
Upgrade and immediately gain expanded capability
Be restricted again if their subscription ends
The system:
Cannot be trivially bypassed
Is resilient to common failure scenarios
Can be modified later without major refactoring
Failure Modes to Avoid
Only enforcing limits in the frontend
Hardcoding limits across multiple files/services
Treating billing state as always reliable/truthful
Not handling downgrade scenarios
Building an overly complex pricing abstraction too early
Notes for Execution
Favor a centralized “entitlement/limits” model rather than scattered checks
Think in terms of:
“What is this user allowed to do?”
Not just “are they premium?”

tasks.title: Don't force workflow linking to single project
tasks.summary: Workflows shouldn't be like only linkable to a single project, they should be available to be used for any project, i.e. when I'm creating a task I shouldn't be limited to a workflows tied to the project the task is for/linked to.

tasks.title: Improve UI/UX of creating tasks
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Improve the UI/UX of task creation in the Agent Management System Prototype so the experience feels more consistent and stable.

## What to change
The current task creation form has too much going on. Rework the experience around progressive disclosure:

- Keep the most commonly accessed inputs and resources readily available.
- Move advanced, detailed, or less frequently needed controls behind drill-down UI rather than exposing everything at once.
- Reduce noise in the default task creation experience.

Include a lightweight **Quick task** path that presents only the bare minimum required to create a task quickly.

Also support a more complete task creation flow for cases that need more detail, collaboration, coordination, approval, or similar process.

## Intended UX principles
- Do not force every task through the full-detail process.
- Make quicker/smaller task creation faster and lower-noise.
- Allow advanced functionality to be exposed progressively when needed.
- Apply these principles consistently across task-creation data entry and related UI/UX.

## Expected deliverable
Produce an implementation or concrete UI/UX improvement to the task creation experience that reflects the principles above.

## Constraints
- Preserve the intent of having both a streamlined path for simple tasks and a fuller path for more involved tasks.
- Do not invent unsupported scope, requirements, files, or product decisions.
- Keep any missing details explicit rather than guessing.

## Review posture
Evaluate the result primarily on whether it makes task creation feel more consistent, stable, and easier to use, especially by reducing unnecessary complexity in the default flow while still allowing deeper control when needed.

## Known gaps
Success criteria, ready conditions, expected outcome details, delegation details, required capabilities, required tools, and requested skills were not provided. If any of those are needed to proceed, surface the gap explicitly instead of assuming.

- how it differs from existing roles
- any uncertainty that cannot be resolved from the available context

## Constraints

- Keep the role definition specific enough to implement without ambiguity.

- clearly scoped
- differentiated from existing roles as far as the available context allows
- ready to implement without ambiguity
- suitable for use in task routing

tasks.title: Review agent management system prototype and do cleanup/pruning
tasks.summary:
Perform an iteration of the app cleanup workflow, based off this recommendation from Codex:

Yes. There are solid patterns for this, and the big idea is:

**Do not ask AI to “clean up the app” in one vague sweep.**
Have it work through a repeatable **prune pipeline** with different classes of garbage separated out:

1. **Definitely unused**
2. **Probably obsolete**
3. **Messy but still live**
4. **Architecturally ugly but currently valuable**

That distinction matters, because AI is decent at removing category 1, somewhat useful on category 2, and dangerous when you let it freestyle on categories 3 and 4.

A good workflow looks more like **detect → classify → prove safe → remove in small batches → verify** than “refactor everything.”

### What to use the machine for first

Start with the most objective cleanup layers before you do subjective cleanup.

For a TypeScript app, the lowest-hanging fruit is usually:

* TypeScript unused locals/parameters via `noUnusedLocals` and `noUnusedParameters`. TypeScript explicitly supports these compiler checks. ([TypeScript][1])
* ESLint rules like `no-unused-vars` and `no-useless-assignment`, which are meant to catch variables and assignments that are never meaningfully used. ([ESLint][2])
* Knip for unused files, unused exports, and unused dependencies. That is pretty much the exact tool category you’re describing. Knip’s docs say it finds unused dependencies, exports, and files by building a project/module graph from actual entry points and framework/tooling knowledge. ([Knip][3])

That gives you an important principle:

**Use static tooling to generate the candidate list. Use AI to triage and execute removals.**

That is much safer than using AI as the detector.

---

## A practical pruning workflow

### Phase 1: Freeze the shape of the current app

Before cleanup, define what “must still work.”

That usually means:

* core user flows
* routes/pages
* major forms/modals
* auth/session behavior
* critical data operations
* any known weird edge cases you already rely on

If you do not define the living surface area of the app, AI will happily delete things that are “unused” in a narrow sense but still relied on by a manual flow, config path, lazy import, background process, or future-near-term feature.

So first create a short “protected surface” doc, something like:

* active routes
* active components
* intentionally dormant experimental areas
* generated files / config files not to touch
* files that are referenced dynamically

This reduces dumb deletions dramatically.

---

### Phase 2: Run objective detectors

For JS/TS apps, your base stack should usually include:

* `tsc --noEmit` with unused checks enabled
* ESLint unused-variable/useless-assignment rules
* Knip for unused files/exports/dependencies ([TypeScript][1])

Why this order?

Because these tools answer different questions:

* **TypeScript**: is this local thing never read?
* **ESLint**: is this variable/assignment pattern pointless or suspicious?
* **Knip**: is this file/export/dependency disconnected from real entry points? ([Knip][3])

That layered view is better than any single tool.

---

### Phase 3: Make AI classify, not immediately delete

Once you have findings, don’t say “remove all of it.”

Instead ask AI to put each finding into buckets like:

* **Safe to remove now**
* **Needs human check because of dynamic usage**
* **Likely dead but part of unfinished feature**
* **Live but should be rewritten**
* **Ignore / false positive**

This matters because static analysis misses things like:

* dynamic imports
* string-based route references
* plugin conventions
* framework magic
* environment-specific entrypoints
* reflection / registry patterns
* code only used in tests, scripts, builds, or generated flows

Knip itself explicitly talks about handling issues via configuration, entry points, project settings, and careful ignore patterns rather than blindly ignoring reports. ([Knip][4])

So the AI’s first job should be **triage**.

---

### Phase 4: Remove in tiny vertical batches

This is the biggest process improvement for AI-heavy prototyping.

Instead of “clean the codebase,” do:

* remove 1 unused dependency
* remove 1 dead utility file cluster
* remove 1 obsolete feature branch worth of components
* remove 1 stale route and its imports
* remove 1 abandoned experiment and its styles/tests/types

Tiny batches are better because:

* easier to verify
* easier to revert
* easier to understand breakage
* easier for AI to reason about causality

The fastest teams often think they need big cleanup passes, but cleanup goes faster when each pass is **narrow and attributable**.

---

### Phase 5: Verify with multiple signals

After each batch, run:

* typecheck
* lint
* tests
* build
* a quick smoke pass of the protected flows

This matters because bundlers can remove dead code through tree shaking, but tree shaking is about final bundle elimination, not necessarily proof that code is safe to delete from the source tree. Rollup’s docs describe tree shaking as elimination of unused code paths in the bundle, which is useful but not the same thing as repository hygiene. Vite uses Rollup in production builds. ([rollupjs.org][5])

So:

**“It doesn’t end up in the bundle” is weaker than “we proved the source is obsolete.”**

---

## The most useful mental model

Think of cleanup as 3 separate jobs:

### 1. Garbage collection

Dead files, dead exports, dead deps, unused variables.

This should be mostly tool-driven.

### 2. Simplification

Collapsing wrappers, deleting indirection, merging duplicated code, reducing prop churn, removing premature abstractions.

This is where AI can help a lot, but only once the obvious dead matter is gone.

### 3. Archaeology

Understanding why weird code exists, whether it was compensating for a real bug, and whether deleting it reintroduces pain.

This is the dangerous part. AI is bad at this unless you constrain it with evidence.

A lot of prototype mess is not “dead code.” It is “scar tissue.”
Deleting scar tissue without understanding the wound is how regressions happen.

---

## A good recurring cadence

For a fast-moving prototype app, I’d usually recommend something like this:

* During rapid prototyping: tolerate some mess, but keep lint/typecheck alive.
* Every few days or after a meaningful feature: run a small prune pass.
* Every 1–2 weeks: run a deeper cleanup pass focused on one area.
* Before releasing or merging major work: run a stricter hygiene pass.

That keeps entropy from compounding.

If you wait too long, the AI-generated sediment layers get harder to separate because more live code starts depending on accidental artifacts.

---

## What to tell AI, specifically

The best prompts are procedural and evidence-driven.

Good pattern:

> Analyze this codebase for unused files, exports, imports, dependencies, dead feature remnants, and stale abstractions.
> Use existing signals first: TypeScript errors, ESLint findings, Knip output, build warnings, route usage, and import graph evidence.
> Classify findings into:
>
> 1. safe to remove now,
> 2. likely dead but needs manual verification,
> 3. live but overcomplicated.
>    Do not delete anything referenced dynamically, by config, by framework conventions, by tests/scripts, or by build tooling unless you can prove it is unused.
>    Propose removals in the smallest coherent batches possible, and for each batch list:
>
> * files to delete,
> * imports/exports to update,
> * risk level,
> * verification steps.

Even better: paste in the actual tool outputs and tell it to work from those.

That changes the task from “guess what’s dead” to “reason over evidence.”

---

## What usually works well in practice

A strong cleanup loop is often:

1. Run detectors.
2. Export findings.
3. Have AI summarize findings by subsystem.
4. Pick one subsystem.
5. Remove one batch.
6. Verify.
7. Commit.
8. Repeat.

So the AI becomes more like a **cleanup operator** than an unbounded refactor goblin.

---

## Common failure modes

A few things to watch for:

### “Unused” but actually runtime-referenced

Especially with framework conventions, dynamic imports, route loading, plugin registration, or string-based lookups.

### Dead code mixed with useful code

AI may remove the whole thing instead of extracting the useful part.

### Removing ugly but essential compatibility glue

Prototype apps often have awkward code that exists because something else is flaky. AI loves deleting this.

### Ignoring dependency-level garbage

Unused npm packages and orphaned helper files quietly rot the repo. Knip is useful here specifically because it targets dependencies, exports, and files, not just local variables. ([Knip][3])

### Doing cleanup too late

Once multiple half-experiments overlap, it becomes archaeology instead of cleanup.

---

## The most effective “best practice” overall

The highest-leverage pattern is:

**Keep exploration cheap, but make consolidation explicit.**

In other words, split your workflow into two modes:

### Exploration mode

* go fast
* tolerate duplication
* tolerate temporary hacks
* leave notes / tags like `experiment`, `temp`, `candidate-delete`

### Consolidation mode

* no new features
* remove unused artifacts
* simplify live paths
* re-state current architecture
* tighten checks

If you blend these constantly, the repo stays muddy.
If you alternate them intentionally, the repo evolves much more cleanly.

---

## If you want the shortest practical version

For your situation, I’d do this:

1. Turn on strict unused checks in TypeScript and ESLint. ([TypeScript][1])
2. Add Knip and run it regularly for unused files/exports/dependencies. ([Knip][3])
3. Treat tool output as the source of truth; use AI to classify and execute, not to guess from scratch.
4. Delete in tiny batches.
5. Verify after every batch with typecheck/lint/build/tests/smoke flows.
6. Maintain a short list of dynamic/framework-magic areas that cleanup should not touch casually.
7. Schedule recurring consolidation passes so prototype sediment does not pile up.

That’s the wheel you probably don’t need to reinvent.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete cleanup workflow for your SvelteKit/Supabase/Vercel setup, including example commands, config ideas, and a Codex prompt you can drop straight into your repo.

[1]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/noUnusedParameters.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "TSConfig Option: noUnusedParameters"
[2]: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/rules/no-unused-vars?utm_source=chatgpt.com "no-unused-vars - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter"
[3]: https://knip.dev/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Knip: Declutter your JavaScript & TypeScript projects"
[4]: https://knip.dev/guides/handling-issues?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Handling Issues"
[5]: https://rollupjs.org/introduction/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Introduction"

That changes the task from “guess what’s dead” to “reason over evidence.”

---

## What usually works well in practice

A strong cleanup loop is often:

1. Run detectors.
2. Export findings.
3. Have AI summarize findings by subsystem.
4. Pick one subsystem.
5. Remove one batch.
6. Verify.
7. Commit.
8. Repeat.

So the AI becomes more like a **cleanup operator** than an unbounded refactor goblin.

---

## Common failure modes

A few things to watch for:

### “Unused” but actually runtime-referenced

Especially with framework conventions, dynamic imports, route loading, plugin registration, or string-based lookups.

### Dead code mixed with useful code

AI may remove the whole thing instead of extracting the useful part.

### Removing ugly but essential compatibility glue

Prototype apps often have awkward code that exists because something else is flaky. AI loves deleting this.

### Ignoring dependency-level garbage

Unused npm packages and orphaned helper files quietly rot the repo. Knip is useful here specifically because it targets dependencies, exports, and files, not just local variables. ([Knip][3])

### Doing cleanup too late

Once multiple half-experiments overlap, it becomes archaeology instead of cleanup.

---

## The most effective “best practice” overall

The highest-leverage pattern is:

**Keep exploration cheap, but make consolidation explicit.**

In other words, split your workflow into two modes:

### Exploration mode

* go fast
* tolerate duplication
* tolerate temporary hacks
* leave notes / tags like `experiment`, `temp`, `candidate-delete`

### Consolidation mode

* no new features
* remove unused artifacts
* simplify live paths
* re-state current architecture
* tighten checks

If you blend these constantly, the repo stays muddy.
If you alternate them intentionally, the repo evolves much more cleanly.

---

## If you want the shortest practical version

For your situation, I’d do this:

1. Turn on strict unused checks in TypeScript and ESLint. ([TypeScript][1])
2. Add Knip and run it regularly for unused files/exports/dependencies. ([Knip][3])
3. Treat tool output as the source of truth; use AI to classify and execute, not to guess from scratch.
4. Delete in tiny batches.
5. Verify after every batch with typecheck/lint/build/tests/smoke flows.
6. Maintain a short list of dynamic/framework-magic areas that cleanup should not touch casually.
7. Schedule recurring consolidation passes so prototype sediment does not pile up.

That’s the wheel you probably don’t need to reinvent.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete cleanup workflow for your SvelteKit/Supabase/Vercel setup, including example commands, config ideas, and a Codex prompt you can drop straight into your repo.

[2]: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/rules/no-unused-vars?utm_source=chatgpt.com "no-unused-vars - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter"
[3]: https://knip.dev/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Knip: Declutter your JavaScript & TypeScript projects"

tasks.title: Review agent management system prototype and do cleanup/pruning: Objective Signal Sweep
tasks.summary:
Run the existing cleanup detectors and technical checks to produce an evidence-backed candidate list of unused files, exports, dependencies, suspicious assignments, stale feature remnants, and other review targets instead of guessing from the tree.

Workflow template: App Review and Cleanup
Parent task: Review agent management system prototype and do cleanup/pruning

delegationPacket.objective: Run the existing cleanup detectors and technical checks to produce an evidence-backed candidate list of unused files, exports, dependencies, suspicious assignments, stale feature remnants, and other review targets instead of guessing from the tree.
delegationPacket.inputContext:
Perform an iteration of the app cleanup workflow, based off this recommendation from Codex:

Yes. There are solid patterns for this, and the big idea is:

**Do not ask AI to “clean up the app” in one vague sweep.**
Have it work through a repeatable **prune pipeline** with different classes of garbage separated out:

1. **Definitely unused**
2. **Probably obsolete**
3. **Messy but still live**
4. **Architecturally ugly but currently valuable**

That distinction matters, because AI is decent at removing category 1, somewhat useful on category 2, and dangerous when you let it freestyle on categories 3 and 4.

A good workflow looks more like **detect → classify → prove safe → remove in small batches → verify** than “refactor everything.”

### What to use the machine for first

Start with the most objective cleanup layers before you do subjective cleanup.

For a TypeScript app, the lowest-hanging fruit is usually:

* TypeScript unused locals/parameters via `noUnusedLocals` and `noUnusedParameters`. TypeScript explicitly supports these compiler checks. ([TypeScript][1])
* ESLint rules like `no-unused-vars` and `no-useless-assignment`, which are meant to catch variables and assignments that are never meaningfully used. ([ESLint][2])
* Knip for unused files, unused exports, and unused dependencies. That is pretty much the exact tool category you’re describing. Knip’s docs say it finds unused dependencies, exports, and files by building a project/module graph from actual entry points and framework/tooling knowledge. ([Knip][3])

That gives you an important principle:

**Use static tooling to generate the candidate list. Use AI to triage and execute removals.**

That is much safer than using AI as the detector.

---

## A practical pruning workflow

### Phase 1: Freeze the shape of the current app

Before cleanup, define what “must still work.”

That usually means:

* core user flows
* routes/pages
* major forms/modals
* auth/session behavior
* critical data operations
* any known weird edge cases you already rely on

If you do not define the living surface area of the app, AI will happily delete things that are “unused” in a narrow sense but still relied on by a manual flow, config path, lazy import, background process, or future-near-term feature.

So first create a short “protected surface” doc, something like:

* active routes
* active components
* intentionally dormant experimental areas
* generated files / config files not to touch
* files that are referenced dynamically

This reduces dumb deletions dramatically.

---

### Phase 2: Run objective detectors

For JS/TS apps, your base stack should usually include:

* `tsc --noEmit` with unused checks enabled
* ESLint unused-variable/useless-assignment rules
* Knip for unused files/exports/dependencies ([TypeScript][1])

Why this order?

Because these tools answer different questions:

* **TypeScript**: is this local thing never read?
* **ESLint**: is this variable/assignment pattern pointless or suspicious?
* **Knip**: is this file/export/dependency disconnected from real entry points? ([Knip][3])

That layered view is better than any single tool.

---

### Phase 3: Make AI classify, not immediately delete

Once you have findings, don’t say “remove all of it.”

Instead ask AI to put each finding into buckets like:

* **Safe to remove now**
* **Needs human check because of dynamic usage**
* **Likely dead but part of unfinished feature**
* **Live but should be rewritten**
* **Ignore / false positive**

This matters because static analysis misses things like:

* dynamic imports
* string-based route references
* plugin conventions
* framework magic
* environment-specific entrypoints
* reflection / registry patterns
* code only used in tests, scripts, builds, or generated flows

Knip itself explicitly talks about handling issues via configuration, entry points, project settings, and careful ignore patterns rather than blindly ignoring reports. ([Knip][4])

So the AI’s first job should be **triage**.

---

### Phase 4: Remove in tiny vertical batches

This is the biggest process improvement for AI-heavy prototyping.

Instead of “clean the codebase,” do:

* remove 1 unused dependency
* remove 1 dead utility file cluster
* remove 1 obsolete feature branch worth of components
* remove 1 stale route and its imports
* remove 1 abandoned experiment and its styles/tests/types

Tiny batches are better because:

* easier to verify
* easier to revert
* easier to understand breakage
* easier for AI to reason about causality

The fastest teams often think they need big cleanup passes, but cleanup goes faster when each pass is **narrow and attributable**.

---

### Phase 5: Verify with multiple signals

After each batch, run:

* typecheck
* lint
* tests
* build
* a quick smoke pass of the protected flows

This matters because bundlers can remove dead code through tree shaking, but tree shaking is about final bundle elimination, not necessarily proof that code is safe to delete from the source tree. Rollup’s docs describe tree shaking as elimination of unused code paths in the bundle, which is useful but not the same thing as repository hygiene. Vite uses Rollup in production builds. ([rollupjs.org][5])

So:

**“It doesn’t end up in the bundle” is weaker than “we proved the source is obsolete.”**

---

## The most useful mental model

Think of cleanup as 3 separate jobs:

### 1. Garbage collection

Dead files, dead exports, dead deps, unused variables.

This should be mostly tool-driven.

### 2. Simplification

Collapsing wrappers, deleting indirection, merging duplicated code, reducing prop churn, removing premature abstractions.

This is where AI can help a lot, but only once the obvious dead matter is gone.

### 3. Archaeology

Understanding why weird code exists, whether it was compensating for a real bug, and whether deleting it reintroduces pain.

This is the dangerous part. AI is bad at this unless you constrain it with evidence.

A lot of prototype mess is not “dead code.” It is “scar tissue.”
Deleting scar tissue without understanding the wound is how regressions happen.

---

## A good recurring cadence

For a fast-moving prototype app, I’d usually recommend something like this:

* During rapid prototyping: tolerate some mess, but keep lint/typecheck alive.
* Every few days or after a meaningful feature: run a small prune pass.
* Every 1–2 weeks: run a deeper cleanup pass focused on one area.
* Before releasing or merging major work: run a stricter hygiene pass.

That keeps entropy from compounding.

If you wait too long, the AI-generated sediment layers get harder to separate because more live code starts depending on accidental artifacts.

---

## What to tell AI, specifically

The best prompts are procedural and evidence-driven.

Good pattern:

> Analyze this codebase for unused files, exports, imports, dependencies, dead feature remnants, and stale abstractions.
> Use existing signals first: TypeScript errors, ESLint findings, Knip output, build warnings, route usage, and import graph evidence.
> Classify findings into:
>
> 1. safe to remove now,
> 2. likely dead but needs manual verification,
> 3. live but overcomplicated.
>    Do not delete anything referenced dynamically, by config, by framework conventions, by tests/scripts, or by build tooling unless you can prove it is unused.
>    Propose removals in the smallest coherent batches possible, and for each batch list:
>
> * files to delete,
> * imports/exports to update,
> * risk level,
> * verification steps.

Even better: paste in the actual tool outputs and tell it to work from those.

That changes the task from “guess what’s dead” to “reason over evidence.”

---

## What usually works well in practice

A strong cleanup loop is often:

1. Run detectors.
2. Export findings.
3. Have AI summarize findings by subsystem.
4. Pick one subsystem.
5. Remove one batch.
6. Verify.
7. Commit.
8. Repeat.

So the AI becomes more like a **cleanup operator** than an unbounded refactor goblin.

---

## Common failure modes

A few things to watch for:

Especially with framework conventions, dynamic imports, route loading, plugin registration, or string-based lookups.

### Dead code mixed with useful code

AI may remove the whole thing instead of extracting the useful part.

### Removing ugly but essential compatibility glue

Prototype apps often have awkward code that exists because something else is flaky. AI loves deleting this.

### Ignoring dependency-level garbage

Unused npm packages and orphaned helper files quietly rot the repo. Knip is useful here specifically because it targets dependencies, exports, and files, not just local variables. ([Knip][3])

### Doing cleanup too late

Once multiple half-experiments overlap, it becomes archaeology instead of cleanup.

---

## The most effective “best practice” overall

The highest-leverage pattern is:

**Keep exploration cheap, but make consolidation explicit.**

In other words, split your workflow into two modes:

### Exploration mode

* go fast
* tolerate duplication
* tolerate temporary hacks
* leave notes / tags like `experiment`, `temp`, `candidate-delete`

### Consolidation mode

* no new features
* remove unused artifacts
* simplify live paths
* re-state current architecture
* tighten checks

If you blend these constantly, the repo stays muddy.
If you alternate them intentionally, the repo evolves much more cleanly.

---

## If you want the shortest practical version

For your situation, I’d do this:

1. Turn on strict unused checks in TypeScript and ESLint. ([TypeScript][1])
2. Add Knip and run it regularly for unused files/exports/dependencies. ([Knip][3])
3. Treat tool output as the source of truth; use AI to classify and execute, not to guess from scratch.
4. Delete in tiny batches.
5. Verify after every batch with typecheck/lint/build/tests/smoke flows.
6. Maintain a short list of dynamic/framework-magic areas that cleanup should not touch casually.
7. Schedule recurring consolidation passes so prototype sediment does not pile up.

That’s the wheel you probably don’t need to reinvent.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete cleanup workflow for your SvelteKit/Supabase/Vercel setup, including example commands, config ideas, and a Codex prompt you can drop straight into your repo.

[1]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/noUnusedParameters.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "TSConfig Option: noUnusedParameters"
[2]: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/rules/no-unused-vars?utm_source=chatgpt.com "no-unused-vars - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter"
[3]: https://knip.dev/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Knip: Declutter your JavaScript & TypeScript projects"

* **Likely dead but part of unfinished feature**
* **Live but should be rewritten**
* **Ignore / false positive**

This matters because static analysis misses things like:

* dynamic imports
* string-based route references
* plugin conventions
* framework magic
* environment-specific entrypoints
* reflection / registry patterns
* code only used in tests, scripts, builds, or generated flows

Knip itself explicitly talks about handling issues via configuration, entry points, project settings, and careful ignore patterns rather than blindly ignoring reports. ([Knip][4])

So the AI’s first job should be **triage**.

---

### Phase 4: Remove in tiny vertical batches

This is the biggest process improvement for AI-heavy prototyping.

Instead of “clean the codebase,” do:

* remove 1 unused dependency
* remove 1 dead utility file cluster
* remove 1 obsolete feature branch worth of components
* remove 1 stale route and its imports
* remove 1 abandoned experiment and its styles/tests/types

Tiny batches are better because:

* easier to verify
* easier to revert
* easier to understand breakage
* easier for AI to reason about causality

The fastest teams often think they need big cleanup passes, but cleanup goes faster when each pass is **narrow and attributable**.

---

### Phase 5: Verify with multiple signals

After each batch, run:

* typecheck
* lint
* tests
* build
* a quick smoke pass of the protected flows

This matters because bundlers can remove dead code through tree shaking, but tree shaking is about final bundle elimination, not necessarily proof that code is safe to delete from the source tree. Rollup’s docs describe tree shaking as elimination of unused code paths in the bundle, which is useful but not the same thing as repository hygiene. Vite uses Rollup in production builds. ([rollupjs.org][5])

So:

**“It doesn’t end up in the bundle” is weaker than “we proved the source is obsolete.”**

---

## The most useful mental model

Think of cleanup as 3 separate jobs:

### 1. Garbage collection

Dead files, dead exports, dead deps, unused variables.

This should be mostly tool-driven.

### 2. Simplification

Collapsing wrappers, deleting indirection, merging duplicated code, reducing prop churn, removing premature abstractions.

This is where AI can help a lot, but only once the obvious dead matter is gone.

### 3. Archaeology

Understanding why weird code exists, whether it was compensating for a real bug, and whether deleting it reintroduces pain.

This is the dangerous part. AI is bad at this unless you constrain it with evidence.

A lot of prototype mess is not “dead code.” It is “scar tissue.”
Deleting scar tissue without understanding the wound is how regressions happen.

---

## A good recurring cadence

For a fast-moving prototype app, I’d usually recommend something like this:

* During rapid prototyping: tolerate some mess, but keep lint/typecheck alive.
* Every few days or after a meaningful feature: run a small prune pass.
* Every 1–2 weeks: run a deeper cleanup pass focused on one area.
* Before releasing or merging major work: run a stricter hygiene pass.

That keeps entropy from compounding.

If you wait too long, the AI-generated sediment layers get harder to separate because more live code starts depending on accidental artifacts.

---

## What to tell AI, specifically

The best prompts are procedural and evidence-driven.

Good pattern:

> Analyze this codebase for unused files, exports, imports, dependencies, dead feature remnants, and stale abstractions.
> Use existing signals first: TypeScript errors, ESLint findings, Knip output, build warnings, route usage, and import graph evidence.
> Classify findings into:
>
> 1. safe to remove now,
> 2. likely dead but needs manual verification,
> 3. live but overcomplicated.
>    Do not delete anything referenced dynamically, by config, by framework conventions, by tests/scripts, or by build tooling unless you can prove it is unused.
>    Propose removals in the smallest coherent batches possible, and for each batch list:
>
> * files to delete,
> * imports/exports to update,
> * risk level,
> * verification steps.

Even better: paste in the actual tool outputs and tell it to work from those.

That changes the task from “guess what’s dead” to “reason over evidence.”

---

## What usually works well in practice

A strong cleanup loop is often:

1. Run detectors.
2. Export findings.
3. Have AI summarize findings by subsystem.
4. Pick one subsystem.
5. Remove one batch.
6. Verify.
7. Commit.
8. Repeat.

So the AI becomes more like a **cleanup operator** than an unbounded refactor goblin.

---

## Common failure modes

A few things to watch for:

### “Unused” but actually runtime-referenced

Especially with framework conventions, dynamic imports, route loading, plugin registration, or string-based lookups.

### Dead code mixed with useful code

AI may remove the whole thing instead of extracting the useful part.

### Removing ugly but essential compatibility glue

Prototype apps often have awkward code that exists because something else is flaky. AI loves deleting this.

### Ignoring dependency-level garbage

Unused npm packages and orphaned helper files quietly rot the repo. Knip is useful here specifically because it targets dependencies, exports, and files, not just local variables. ([Knip][3])

### Doing cleanup too late

Once multiple half-experiments overlap, it becomes archaeology instead of cleanup.

---

## The most effective “best practice” overall

The highest-leverage pattern is:

**Keep exploration cheap, but make consolidation explicit.**

In other words, split your workflow into two modes:

### Exploration mode

* go fast
* tolerate duplication
* tolerate temporary hacks
* leave notes / tags like `experiment`, `temp`, `candidate-delete`

### Consolidation mode

* no new features
* remove unused artifacts
* simplify live paths
* re-state current architecture
* tighten checks

If you blend these constantly, the repo stays muddy.
If you alternate them intentionally, the repo evolves much more cleanly.

---

## If you want the shortest practical version

For your situation, I’d do this:

1. Turn on strict unused checks in TypeScript and ESLint. ([TypeScript][1])
2. Add Knip and run it regularly for unused files/exports/dependencies. ([Knip][3])
3. Treat tool output as the source of truth; use AI to classify and execute, not to guess from scratch.
4. Delete in tiny batches.
5. Verify after every batch with typecheck/lint/build/tests/smoke flows.
6. Maintain a short list of dynamic/framework-magic areas that cleanup should not touch casually.
7. Schedule recurring consolidation passes so prototype sediment does not pile up.

That’s the wheel you probably don’t need to reinvent.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete cleanup workflow for your SvelteKit/Supabase/Vercel setup, including example commands, config ideas, and a Codex prompt you can drop straight into your repo.

[1]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/noUnusedParameters.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "TSConfig Option: noUnusedParameters"
[2]: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/rules/no-unused-vars?utm_source=chatgpt.com "no-unused-vars - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter"
[3]: https://knip.dev/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Knip: Declutter your JavaScript & TypeScript projects"
[4]: https://knip.dev/guides/handling-issues?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Handling Issues"
[5]: https://rollupjs.org/introduction/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Introduction"

tasks.summary: The Toast component UI is kinda broken. The text isn't visible when a Toast pops up. Also, on mobile/smaller screens the Toast covers the bottom nav bar/menu, which is poor UX. So the UI/UX of the Toast component needs to be fixed/improved so that it is actually useful and polished

tasks.title: Improve organization of output artifacts
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Review how artifacts and outputs from tasks and threads are currently organized in the Agent Management System prototype, then identify clearer organization and information-architecture options.

## Context
The current setup appears messy:
- agents sometimes put artifacts in inconsistent locations
- much of the output is dumped into `agent_output/`

## Work
1. Inspect the existing structure for task and thread artifacts/output.
2. Identify the main problems in the current organization, especially inconsistent placement and overuse of `agent_output/`.
3. Develop a small set of better organization options based on what actually exists.
4. Compare the options on clarity, consistency, maintainability, and how likely agents are to follow them reliably.
5. Keep any missing information or uncertainty explicit rather than guessing.

## Expected Deliverable
Provide a concise recommendation that includes:
- a summary of the current organizational issues you observed
- potential organization / information-architecture options
- tradeoffs for each option
- your recommended direction and why
- any open questions or missing decisions that would affect the final choice

## Constraints
- Preserve the current intent: improve organization of output artifacts for tasks and threads.
- Do not invent scope, files, structures, or decisions that are not supported by the current system state.
- Treat this as an information-organization problem, not just a folder cleanup exercise.
- Prefer practical options that could be executed later by an agent.

tasks.title: Create financial model for Kwipoo
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Create a clear plan for researching, building, and iteratively improving a financial model for Kwipoo.

This model should help assess Kwipoo's financial viability and scenario outcomes, including:
- the cost to run and distribute Kwipoo
- expected revenue and related assumptions
- how different assumptions change the outlook

Keep the broader business context in view: the product goal is to get a paying Kwipoo customer.

# What to Produce
Deliver a concise execution-ready plan that covers:
- the key questions the financial model must answer
- the research needed before the first usable version can be built
- the inputs the model will require, grouped by category
- the major cost areas to estimate
- the major revenue drivers to estimate
- the scenarios/sensitivities that should be tested
- an iterative build sequence for improving the model over time
- the most important uncertainties, assumptions, and missing information

# Constraints
- Preserve uncertainty where inputs are missing; do not guess silently.
- Do research and analysis appropriate to support the plan, but do not invent unsupported facts, metrics, or decisions.
- Keep the output focused on building and improving the financial model, not on unrelated business strategy.
- No specific files, tools, or repositories are currently mandated.

# Working Guidance
- Start by defining the minimum useful version of the model for judging viability.
- Separate fixed assumptions, variable assumptions, and currently unknown inputs.
- Include both baseline and alternative scenarios so the model can support decision-making.
- Make clear which estimates are likely to matter most for viability.
- If important information is unavailable, call that out explicitly and show how the model should handle it until better data exists.

# Review Posture
Optimize for something an execution agent can act on immediately:
- structured
- concrete
- assumption-aware
- easy to extend in later iterations

tasks.title: Determine initial monetization model
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Determine the strongest initial monetization model for **Kwipoo** by researching and analyzing monetization approaches that could be viable for Kwipoo and its market.

## Deliverable
Produce a concise analysis that includes:
- A short summary of the product and market context you were able to confirm for Kwipoo.
- A set of viable monetization options for Kwipoo.
- A comparison of those options with clear pros, risks, and fit for Kwipoo.
- Your recommended **initial** monetization model, with a clear rationale.
- Any key assumptions, uncertainties, or missing information that limit confidence.

## Constraints
- Preserve uncertainty explicitly. If Kwipoo's product details, audience, or market context are unclear, say so instead of guessing.
- Do not invent scope, requirements, files, or business decisions that are not supported by the available context.
- Focus on analysis and planning. This task is to determine viable monetization models, not to implement anything.
- Treat "best" as a reasoned recommendation based on the evidence you can gather, not as a claim of certainty.

## Approach
1. Gather the minimum necessary context to understand what Kwipoo is, who it serves, and what market it operates in.
2. Identify monetization models that are plausibly viable for Kwipoo and its market.
3. Evaluate each option against Kwipoo's apparent product shape, user value, likely buying behavior, and practical go-to-market fit.
4. Recommend the most defensible initial model and explain why it should come first.
5. Call out what should be validated next if the available information is incomplete.

## Review Posture
Be analytical and decision-oriented. Favor clear tradeoffs over broad brainstorming, and keep unsupported assumptions visible.

tasks.title: Add move-another-here continuation for Kwipoo destination-first organizing sessions
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Make repeated organizing sessions lighter by offering a continuation path after a successful move into a destination.

## What To Do
- Reuse the existing destination-first batch move pattern where possible.
- After a successful move, offer a lightweight way to move another item into the same destination.
- Keep the chosen destination sticky during the continuation flow.
- Favor a simple continuation prompt over a heavy new session mode unless the codebase clearly needs more.

## Constraints
- Do not redesign all move entry points in one task.
- Keep the implementation incremental and aligned with the current batch move architecture.
- Make assumptions explicit where the ideal continuation surface is still uncertain.

## Expected Deliverable
- A concrete move-another-here continuation path for destination-first move sessions.
- A concise summary of the chosen interaction model, reuse points, and any remaining gaps for larger organizing sessions.
tasks.successCriteria: Users can continue moving multiple items into the same destination with less repeated navigation, and the implementation fits the existing destination-first batch architecture.
tasks.readyCondition: Post-move confirmation and undo are complete, and the current move architecture has enough clarity to extend destination-first continuation safely.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Common organizing sessions require fewer repeated destination-picking steps and feel more fluid.

tasks.title: Add post-move confirmation and undo to the Kwipoo move flow
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Improve trust in move flows by replacing the generic move-success experience with clearer confirmation and an immediate undo path.

## What To Do
- Replace the generic success feedback with clearer from-to confirmation for moves.
- Add a short-lived undo action that restores the previous spot using explicit move behavior.
- Make pending or syncing state visible enough that users understand what happened when offline or reconnecting.

## Constraints
- Do not hide sync failures behind magical rollback behavior.
- Keep undo compatible with the current local-first move model.
- Preserve clarity over cleverness.

## Expected Deliverable
- A concrete post-move confirmation and undo flow.
- A concise summary of how undo works, what edge cases were handled, and any remaining sync-related risks.
tasks.successCriteria: Users can immediately understand where an item was moved, reverse an accidental move quickly, and the behavior remains correct while offline or syncing.
tasks.readyCondition: The ranking task is complete and the move flow has enough instrumentation and destination clarity to support safe recovery behavior.
tasks.expectedOutcome: The move flow feels more trustworthy and better at preventing silent location drift after a quick or mistaken move.

tasks.title: Add recent destinations and deterministic ranking to Kwipoo move flows
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Reduce repeated browsing in the single-item move flow by ranking likely destinations higher, starting with recent destinations and explicit deterministic rules.

## What To Do
- Introduce a small recents model for successful move destinations.
- Add a visible recent-destinations section or similarly explicit ranking treatment rather than silently making the list unpredictable.
- Use deterministic ranking rules such as current-session recents, durable recents, sibling-context clues, and alphabetical fallback.
- Keep the ranking behavior understandable enough that users can still predict where items will appear.

## Constraints
- Do not jump to a synced cross-device ranking model unless the implementation clearly requires it.
- Keep uncertainty explicit where ranking rules are still product judgment.
- Preserve current permissions and visibility behavior.

## Expected Deliverable
- A first-pass recent-destinations and ranking implementation for the move flow.
- A concise summary of the ranking rules, storage choice, and any trust or predictability risks that remain.
tasks.successCriteria: Recent or likely destinations are surfaced in a deterministic, understandable way, repeat moves take less browsing effort, and the change does not make the list feel arbitrary.
tasks.readyCondition: Destination search and path disambiguation are complete so ranking builds on a clear destination model instead of compensating for ambiguity.
tasks.expectedOutcome: Repeat moves can often be completed from a short top section instead of broad scanning.

tasks.title: Workflows
tasks.summary:
You are reviewing and extending an existing system that already includes tasks and roles. The missing layer is workflows.

Your job is to review the current system and propose the smallest useful workflow model that fits the existing architecture without unnecessary abstraction or enterprise-style overengineering.

Context:

* We already have tasks.
* We already have roles.
* We do not yet have workflows as a first-class concept.
* The goal is to add a workflow layer that coordinates tasks across time, dependencies, roles, and execution paths.
* The system should support both repeatable processes and messy/adaptive work.
* Do not replace tasks or roles. Workflows should sit above or around them.

Design intent:

* A task is a unit of work.
* A role is a capability profile suited for certain tasks.
* A workflow is a structure that links tasks together into a process.
* Workflows should make it easier to represent sequencing, dependencies, parallel work, routing, and overall process state.
* The system must remain flexible enough for exploratory work, not just rigid predefined pipelines.

What I want from you:

1. Review the current implementation of tasks and roles.
2. Identify any existing fields, patterns, or assumptions that already act like implicit workflows.
3. Propose a minimal workflow model that integrates with the current system.
4. Explain the tradeoffs of your design.
5. Show how the model would work using at least 3 concrete examples:

   * a simple linear workflow
   * a workflow with parallel tasks
   * a workflow with adaptive branching or uncertainty
6. Recommend a phased implementation plan, starting with the smallest useful version.
7. Highlight anything in the current schema or code that should be reused, changed, or avoided.

Constraints:

* Prefer simple, explicit models over highly abstract orchestration frameworks.
* Do not introduce a complex workflow engine unless clearly justified.
* Do not build a universal DSL.
* Avoid premature automation.
* Preserve human override and manual adjustment.
* Assume the system may later involve AI agents, but do not design around speculative future complexity.
* Optimize for clarity, maintainability, and real usefulness in a small-team environment.

Questions to answer:

* What is the minimal workflow entity or set of entities we need?
* Should workflows contain tasks directly, or should there be workflow-task instances?
* How should dependencies be represented?
* How should parallelizable work be modeled?
* How should branching or follow-up task creation work in the early version?
* What lifecycle states should a workflow have?
* How should workflows interact with roles and assignment?
* What should remain manual in v1?

Output format:

* First: summary of current system findings
* Second: proposed conceptual model
* Third: recommended schema / type changes
* Fourth: concrete examples
* Fifth: phased implementation plan
* Sixth: risks / overengineering traps to avoid

Important:
Do not drift into vague theory. Ground everything in the existing system and show practical implementation detail.
tasks.successCriteria: Instructions have been fulfilled
tasks.readyCondition: Instructions have been fulfilled
tasks.expectedOutcome: Instructions have been fulfilled

## Review Posture

tasks.title: Ability to give other users/people things, places, sets.
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Assess how Kwipoo could support users giving or transferring `things`, `places`, and `sets` to other users/people, and produce a practical implementation plan.

This work is for planning and review, not automatic implementation. The underlying user goal is that my Grandma and I can consistently use Kwipoo, especially when real-life ownership changes should be reflected in the app.

## Product intent to preserve
- `Things` are the clearest case: if I give someone an item/thing in real life, I want the app to reflect that exchange of ownership.
- `Places` may be transferred less often, but this capability could still be useful.
- `Sets` may also be transferred less often, but should be evaluated as part of the same problem.
- The first version should stay simple.
- A more advanced future version might include transaction/transfer/exchange records, but that future scope should stay explicit and not be assumed for the initial plan.

## What to do
1. Review the current codebase and product structure to understand how `things`, `places`, `sets`, users/people, and ownership currently work.
2. Analyze whether these three entity types should share one transfer model or require different handling.
3. Identify the smallest useful first version that supports ownership transfer without over-designing the feature.
4. Note likely follow-on enhancements only if they are directly relevant to the design, such as transaction/transfer/exchange history.

## Expected deliverable
Return a concise planning note in markdown that includes:
- A brief summary of the current state as it relates to ownership and transfer.
- Key product and technical considerations for enabling transfers between users/people.
- A recommended v1 approach for `things`, `places`, and `sets`, including where they should behave the same or differently.
- A phased implementation plan.

tasks.title: Improve UI and UX of managing permissions and sharing of data/objects
tasks.summary:
# Objective
Improve the UI and UX for managing permissions and sharing of data/objects in the Kwipoo app so the product feels more viable as a regular household inventory tool.

## Focus Area
The current experience for managing who can view or edit things/places/spots/etc. is not working well.

Problems already identified:
- The UX is not good.
- The UI is not good.
- The current approach does not feel intuitive.
- The current presentation is not visually appealing.
- A table-like presentation may be a better fit.
- The permissions/sharing section may benefit from expand/collapse behavior.
- The exact desired interaction model is not fully specified, so use judgment and keep assumptions explicit.

## Execution Guidance
- Improve the permissions/sharing management experience within the existing product context.
- Prioritize clarity, scanability, and ease of understanding when deciding how people view and edit access.
- Explore table-like layouts and expandable/collapsible structure if they meaningfully improve usability.
- Keep changes aligned with existing project patterns and avoid unnecessary refactors.
- Do not guess missing product requirements; where the draft is ambiguous, make the ambiguity explicit in your reasoning and output.

## Skills And Roles
- Use the appropriate skills available in the workspace.
- Create any roles needed with the `role-creator` agent skill if that skill is available in the environment.
- If the needed role tooling is unavailable, state that explicitly rather than inventing unsupported process.

## Expected Deliverable
- A concrete improvement to the permissions/sharing UI and UX.
- A concise summary of what changed, what assumptions were required, and any remaining uncertainty because the desired behavior was not fully specified.

## Review Posture
Evaluate the result primarily against:
- Intuitiveness of managing who can view or edit items.
- Visual clarity and overall appeal.
- Whether the structure feels more organized and easier to scan than the current version.
- Whether the chosen pattern supports managing permissions across things/places/spots/etc. without feeling confusing or awkward.

tasks.title: Ensure creating a Kwipoo account works
tasks.summary:
## Objective
Ensure new Kwipoo account creation works reliably for a real customer, starting with email signup and including any other account-creation path that already exists.

This task is about reducing the chance that signup is broken in any way and building as much confidence as realistically possible that the flow works consistently.

## What To Do
- Identify every existing way a user can create a Kwipoo account. Email signup is definitely in scope; include other paths only if they already exist.
- Verify the full account-creation experience end to end for each path you find.
- Find and fix issues that would prevent or degrade successful account creation.
- Improve the ability to test this reliably so the team can detect breakage with confidence going forward.

## Execution Constraints
- Do not assume unconfirmed signup methods, requirements, or success criteria. If something is missing or unclear, keep that explicit.
- Favor practical reliability over broad refactors.
- Preserve existing patterns unless a change is necessary to make signup work or make it testable.
- Review this task with a reliability and regression-prevention posture, not just a narrow bug-fix posture.
- If you make a user-facing product change, add a Changeset per repo policy.

## Expected Deliverable
Deliver a working, confidence-building improvement to account creation that includes:
- the fixes needed to make signup work reliably
- a reliable way to test the experience
- a concise summary of what was verified, what was changed, and any remaining uncertainty or risk

## Done When
- Email-based account creation works in a repeatable end-to-end test.
- Any other existing account-creation path has been checked and either verified working or fixed.
- There is a clear, reliable way to re-run validation and catch future breakage.
- Any remaining gaps are called out explicitly instead of assumed away.

tasks.title: Create roles
tasks.summary:
Create roles in the Agent Management system for each of the below described:

The Structure You’re Reaching For (Refined)

What you’re describing becomes much clearer if you think of it as a pipeline:

1. Capability → 2. Consequence → 3. Relevance → 4. Translation → 5. Delivery

Each of these can map to an agent role.

1. Capability Extractor (Product Truth Layer)

This is your “objective-ish” agent.

Its job is NOT:

“What value does this have?”

Its job IS:

“What does this actually do, in precise, functional terms?”

Example (Kwipoo-ish):

Stores structured data about physical items
Links items to locations
Tracks ownership and metadata
Enables retrieval of item information
Allows grouping (sets)

This is mechanical truth, not marketing.

2. Consequence Mapper (Latent Value Layer)

This is where “value” actually starts to emerge.

It asks:

“If someone uses this capability, what changes in reality?”

Examples:

Less time spent searching for items
Reduced cognitive load
Better insurance documentation
Faster packing/moving
Less duplicate purchasing
Increased awareness of owned assets

This is cause → effect, still not persona-specific.

3. Relevance Matcher (Market Discovery Layer)

Now you bring in humans.

This agent asks:

“Who experiences these problems strongly enough to care?”

Examples:

Frequent movers → high friction in item tracking
Collectors → high value in cataloging
Homeowners → insurance + asset tracking
Travelers → packing + gear management
ADHD / disorganized individuals → cognitive load reduction

This is where your earlier idea of “latent problem vs aware problem” becomes critical:

Some groups feel pain but don’t name it
Others actively search for solutions

This agent should ideally rank:

Pain intensity
Awareness level
Willingness to act
4. Context Translator (Persona Specialist Layer)

This is the part you were intuitively reaching for with “pull in specialists.”

Each specialist answers:

“How does THIS group interpret the same underlying value?”

Same core capability → different framing:

Collector:

“Catalog and track your collection with precision”

Homeowner:

“Know what you own for insurance and emergencies”

Traveler:

“Never forget gear or overpack again”

ADHD user:

“Stop losing track of your stuff and reduce mental clutter”

Nothing about the product changed—only interpretation.

5. Activation Strategist (Go-to-Market Layer)

Now it becomes:

“Where and how do we reach them?”

This includes:

Channels (Reddit, TikTok, niche forums, etc.)
Format (short video, long-form, landing pages)
Hooks (problem-first vs aspiration-first)

tasks.title: Trace Kwipoo route data loading and sync overhead
tasks.summary: Review /places, /things, and /profile after-mount fetch behavior, payload scope, offline hydration, and server endpoint cost. Focus on duplicate or overly broad collection fetches, IndexedDB hydration and merge work, and the Supabase query shape behind collection and shared-data endpoints.
tasks.successCriteria: Produce a concise evidence note that identifies the highest-cost route data flows, where over-fetching or sync overhead appears likely, and which endpoint or hydration path should be optimized first.
tasks.readyCondition: The parent performance plan exists and the Kwipoo app routes and endpoints are available for code and runtime tracing.
tasks.expectedOutcome: A clear map of the route-loading and sync paths most likely to slow core inventory flows.

delegationPacket.objective: Quantify how much route data loading, hydration, and server query work is happening on the core authenticated flows so the parent task can target the biggest real bottleneck.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the parent performance plan and focus on /places, /things, /profile, the things and places page controllers, offline hydration/merge paths, and collection/shared-data endpoints.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A markdown note with request traces, payload observations, sync-path findings, endpoint hotspots, and the highest-confidence optimization targets.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: Core route data flows have been traced, likely duplicate or broad fetches have been identified, and the most important endpoint or sync costs are documented with evidence.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Highlight which data-loading or sync issue most directly affects the experience of a serious returning user managing real inventory.

tasks.title: Audit Kwipoo background work and scaling risks
tasks.summary: Profile idle and background behavior on authenticated routes, then review realtime subscriptions, connectivity polling, chat summary derivation, and interaction/render costs on populated screens. The goal is to identify battery, idle CPU/network, and scaling risks that could make the app feel heavy or unreliable over time.
tasks.successCriteria: Produce a concise evidence note covering idle timers/listeners/subscriptions, chat or notification scaling risks, and the most important background or render-performance issues to validate next.
tasks.readyCondition: The parent performance plan exists and the Kwipoo app can be profiled in an authenticated state.
tasks.expectedOutcome: A ranked list of background and scaling risks that matter after startup, especially for chat-heavy or larger-data accounts.

delegationPacket.objective: Identify non-startup performance risks that could still hurt trust, battery, or responsiveness for a paying customer who uses the app regularly.
delegationPacket.inputContext: Use the parent performance plan and focus on connectivity polling, realtime subscriptions, chat derivation logic, and render/interaction costs on collection-heavy screens.
delegationPacket.expectedDeliverable: A markdown note with idle-profile findings, scaling risks, and recommended next actions for background activity and render performance.
delegationPacket.doneCondition: Idle/background behavior has been inspected, the most credible scaling risks are documented, and the parent task can compare them against startup and data-loading priorities.
delegationPacket.integrationNotes: Flag anything that looks especially risky for wrapper-app battery life or for accounts with enough messages or inventory to become real customers.

tasks.title: Clean up documentation content
